1	---
2	name: golang
3	description: >
4	  Go coding standards and conventions for this project. Apply when writing,
5	  reviewing, or refactoring any Go source file.
6	triggers:
7	  - on_commit
8	---
9	
10	# Go Development Instructions
11	
12	Follow idiomatic Go practices and community standards when writing Go code.
13	These instructions are based on [Effective Go](https://go.dev/doc/effective_go),
14	[Go Code Review Comments](https://go.dev/wiki/CodeReviewComments),
15	and [Google's Go Style Guide](https://google.github.io/styleguide/go/).
16	
17	## General Instructions
18	
19	- Write simple, clear, and idiomatic Go code
20	- Favor clarity and simplicity over cleverness
21	- Follow the principle of least surprise
22	- Keep the happy path left-aligned (reduce indentation)
23	- Return early to reduce nesting
24	- Make the zero value useful
25	- Document exported types, functions, methods, and packages
26	- Use Go modules for dependency management
27	- **AVOID `else` statements - use early returns, continue, or break instead**
28	- Avoid wrapping primitives without a clear semantic benefit; define new types when they add meaning.
29	- Use typed slices/maps and document element semantics when not obvious.
30	- Start error strings with a lowercase letter.
31	
32	## Naming Conventions
33	
34	### Packages
35	
36	- Use lowercase, single-word package names
37	- Avoid `_` characters, hyphens, or mixedCaps
38	- Choose names that describe what the package provides, not what it contains
39	- Avoid generic names like `util`, `common`, or `base`
40	- Package names should be singular, not plural
41	
42	### Variables and Functions
43	
44	- Use mixedCaps or MixedCaps (camelCase) rather than `_` characters
45	- Keep names short but descriptive
46	- Use single-letter variables for very short scopes (like loop indices)
47	- Exported names start with a capital letter
48	- Unexported names start with a lowercase letter
49	- Avoid stuttering (e.g., avoid `http.HTTPServer`, prefer `http.Server`)
50	
51	### Interfaces
52	
53	- Name interfaces with -er suffix when possible (e.g., `Reader`, `Writer`, `Formatter`)
54	- Single-method interfaces should be named after the method (e.g., `Read` → `Reader`)
55	- Keep interfaces small and focused
56	
57	### Constants
58	
59	- Use MixedCaps for exported constants
60	- Use mixedCaps for unexported constants
61	- Group related constants using `const` blocks
62	- Consider using typed constants for better type safety
63	
64	## Code Style and Formatting
65	
66	### Formatting
67	
68	- Always use `gofmt` to format code
69	- Use `goimports` to manage imports automatically
70	- Keep line to 180 max at all times
71	- Add blank lines to separate logical groups of code
72	
73	### Comments
74	
75	- Write comments in complete sentences
76	- Start sentences with the name of the thing being described
77	- Package comments should start with "Package [name]"
78	- Use line comments (`//`) for most comments
79	- Use block comments (`/* */`) sparingly, mainly for package documentation
80	- Document why, not what, unless the what is complex
81	
82	### Error Handling
83	
84	- Check errors immediately after the function call
85	- Don't ignore errors using `_` unless you have a good reason (document why)
86	- Wrap errors with context using `fmt.Errorf` with `%w` verb
87	- Create custom error types when you need to check for specific errors
88	- Place error returns as the last return value
89	- Name error variables `err`
90	- Keep error messages lowercase and don't end with punctuation
91	
92	### Logging
93	
94	- Always use the codebase `log` package for logging
95	- Log errors at the point they occur using `log.Error(err)`
96	- Do not format the errors, let the `log` package handle it
97	- For complex function calls, use `defer log.Trace(time.Now(), args)`
98	    where args are the function arguments at the start of the function.
99	
100	### Control Flow
101	
102	- **NEVER use `else` statements** - they create unnecessary nesting and reduce readability
103	- Use early returns to handle error cases and edge conditions first
104	- Use `continue` in loops to skip to the next iteration instead of nesting
105	- Use `break` to exit loops early instead of complex conditional logic
106	- Keep the main logic (happy path) left-aligned with minimal indentation
107	
108	**❌ BAD - Don't do this:**
109	
110	```go
111	func processEntry(entry *Entry) string {
112	    if entry.Expired() {
113	        return "expired"
114	    } else {
115	        if entry.TTL < 0 {
116	            return "never expires"
117	        } else {
118	            return fmt.Sprintf("expires at %s", time.Unix(entry.Timestamp, 0))
119	        }
120	    }
121	}
122	```
123	
124	**✅ GOOD - Do this instead:**
125	
126	```go
127	func processEntry(entry *Entry) string {
128	    if entry.Expired() {
129	        return "expired"
130	    }
131	
132	    if entry.TTL < 0 {
133	        return "never expires"
134	    }
135	
136	    return fmt.Sprintf("expires at %s", time.Unix(entry.Timestamp, 0))
137	}
138	```
139	
140	**❌ BAD - Nested loop logic:**
141	
142	```go
143	for _, item := range items {
144	    if item.IsValid() {
145	        if item.ShouldProcess() {
146	            // complex processing logic
147	        }
148	    }
149	}
150	```
151	
152	**✅ GOOD - Early continue:**
153	
154	```go
155	for _, item := range items {
156	    if !item.IsValid() {
157	        continue
158	    }
159	    if !item.ShouldProcess() {
160	        continue
161	    }
162	
163	    // complex processing logic (happy path)
164	}
165	```
166	
167	## Architecture and Project Structure
168	
169	### Package Organization
170	
171	- Follow standard Go project layout conventions
172	- Group related functionality into packages
173	- Avoid circular dependencies
174	
175	### Dependency Management
176	
177	- Use Go modules (`go.mod` and `go.sum`)
178	- Keep dependencies minimal
179	- Regularly update dependencies for security patches
180	- Use `go mod tidy` to clean up unused dependencies
181	- Vendor dependencies when necessary
182	
183	## Type Safety and Language Features
184	
185	### Type Definitions
186	
187	- Define types to add meaning and type safety
188	- Use struct tags for JSON, YAML and TOML on exported fields
189	- Prefer explicit type conversions
190	- Use type assertions carefully and check the second return value
191	
192	### Pointers vs Values
193	
194	- Use pointers for large structs or when you need to modify the receiver
195	- Use values for small structs and when immutability is desired
196	- Be consistent within a type's method set
197	- Consider the zero value when choosing pointer vs value receivers
198	
199	### Interfaces and Composition
200	
201	- Accept interfaces, return concrete types
202	- Keep interfaces small (1-3 methods is ideal)
203	- Use embedding for composition
204	- Define interfaces close to where they're used, not where they're implemented
205	- Don't export interfaces unless necessary
206	
207	## Concurrency
208	
209	### Goroutines
210	
211	- Don't create goroutines in libraries; let the caller control concurrency
212	- Always know how a goroutine will exit
213	- Use `sync.WaitGroup` or channels to wait for goroutines
214	- Avoid goroutine leaks by ensuring cleanup
215	
216	### Channels
217	
218	- Use channels to communicate between goroutines
219	- Don't communicate by sharing memory; share memory by communicating
220	- Close channels from the sender side, not the receiver
221	- Use buffered channels when you know the capacity
222	- Use `select` for non-blocking operations
223	
224	### Synchronization
225	
226	- Use `sync.Mutex` for protecting shared state
227	- Keep critical sections small
228	- Use `sync.RWMutex` when you have many readers
229	- Prefer channels over mutexes when possible
230	- Use `sync.Once` for one-time initialization
231	
232	## Error Handling Patterns
233	
234	### Creating Errors
235	
236	- Use `errors.New` for simple static errors
237	- Use `fmt.Errorf` for errors with runtime values
238	- Create custom error types for domain-specific errors
239	- Export error variables for sentinel errors
240	- Use `errors.Is` and `errors.As` for error checking
241	
242	### Error Propagation
243	
244	- Add context when propagating errors up the stack
245	- Don't log and return errors (choose one)
246	- Handle errors at the appropriate level
247	- Consider using structured errors for better debugging
248	
249	## Performance Optimization
250	
251	### Memory Management
252	
253	- Minimize allocations in hot paths
254	- Reuse objects when possible (consider `sync.Pool`)
255	- Use value receivers for small structs
256	- Preallocate slices when size is known
257	- Avoid unnecessary string conversions
258	
259	### Profiling
260	
261	- Use built-in profiling tools (`pprof`)
262	- Benchmark critical code paths
263	- Profile before making performance changes
264	- Focus on algorithmic improvements first
265	- Consider using `testing.B` for benchmarks
266	
267	## Testing
268	
269	### Test Organization
270	
271	- Keep tests in the same package (white-box testing)
272	- Use `_test` package suffix for black-box testing
273	- Name test files with `_test.go` suffix
274	- Place test files next to the code they test
275	
276	### Writing Tests
277	
278	- Name tests descriptively using `TestFunctionNameScenario`
279	- Use subtests with `t.Run` for better organization
280	- Test both success and error cases
281	- Use `testify/assert` and `testify/require` for assertions
282	- Include both positive and negative test cases
283	- Test edge cases and error conditions
284	- When including a standard library that conflicts with an existing import,
285	  use the lib(library name) pattern to avoid conflicts.
286	  Such as: `libtime` for the `time` package.
287	
288	#### Table-driven tests are the default
289	
290	One behavior under test = one test function with a table of cases. Never write several
291	near-identical test functions that differ only in input data, fixtures, or expected outcome —
292	those differences are table fields. A per-case fixture (a different map, config, or mock return)
293	is not a reason to split; put the fixture in the table. Shared setup (mocks, caches, `Init`
294	calls) runs once before the loop.
295	
296	When adding cases to an existing test file, extend the existing table instead of adding a new
297	test function.
298	
299	Only split into separate test functions when the flow genuinely differs: a different API under
300	test, or a setup/assertion sequence that cannot be expressed as table fields.
301	
302	```go
303	// ✅ CORRECT: fixture and error expectation are table fields
304	cases := []struct {
305	    Fixture       Palette
306	    Case          string
307	    Input         Ansi
308	    Expected      Ansi
309	    ExpectedError bool
310	}{
311	    {Case: "literal", Fixture: Palette{"a": "#123456"}, Input: "p:a", Expected: "#123456"},
312	    {Case: "invalid", Fixture: Palette{"a": "{{ broken"}, Input: "p:a", ExpectedError: true},
313	}
314	
315	// ❌ WRONG: TestResolveLiteral, TestResolveReference, TestResolveInvalid —
316	// three functions repeating the same setup with different data
317	```
318	
319	### Test Helpers
320	
321	- Mark helper functions with `t.Helper()`
322	- Create test fixtures for complex setup
323	- Use `testing.TB` interface for functions used in tests and benchmarks
324	- Clean up resources using `t.Cleanup()`
325	
326	#### Global state: always save the original value and restore it
327	
328	When a test mutates package-level variables (resolvers, loggers, clocks, `time.Local`, etc.),
329	save the original value into a local variable and restore it via `t.Cleanup`. Never restore to a
330	hardcoded value; you would overwrite whatever state preceded your test.
331	
332	```go
333	// ✅ CORRECT: save original, restore original
334	origResolver := myPackageResolver
335	t.Cleanup(func() { myPackageResolver = origResolver })
336	myPackageResolver = fakeResolver
337	
338	origLocal := time.Local
339	t.Cleanup(func() { time.Local = origLocal })
340	time.Local = time.UTC
341	
342	// ❌ WRONG: restores to a hardcoded value instead of the pre-test value
343	defer func() { time.Local = time.FixedZone("UTC", 0) }()
344	```
345	
346	## Security Best Practices
347	
348	### Input Validation
349	
350	- Validate all external input
351	- Use strong typing to prevent invalid states
352	- Sanitize data before using in SQL queries
353	- Be careful with file paths from user input
354	- Validate and escape data for different contexts (HTML, SQL, shell)
355	
356	### Cryptography
357	
358	- Use standard library crypto packages
359	- Never write your own cryptography
360	- Use crypto/rand for random number generation
361	- Store passwords using bcrypt or similar
362	- Use TLS for network communication
363	
364	## Documentation
365	
366	### Code Documentation
367	
368	- Document all exported symbols
369	- Start documentation with the symbol name
370	- Use examples in documentation when helpful
371	- Keep documentation close to code
372	- Update documentation when code changes
373	
374	### README and Documentation Files
375	
376	- Include clear setup instructions
377	- Document dependencies and requirements
378	- Provide usage examples
379	- Document configuration options
380	- Include troubleshooting section
381	
382	## Tools and Development Workflow
383	
384	### Essential Tools
385	
386	- `go fmt`: Format code
387	- `go vet`: Find suspicious constructs
388	- `golint` or `golangci-lint`: Additional linting
389	- `go test`: Run tests
390	- `go mod`: Manage dependencies
391	- `go generate`: Code generation
392	
393	### Development Practices
394	
395	- Run tests before committing
396	- Use pre-commit hooks for formatting and linting
397	- Keep commits focused and atomic
398	- Write clear, descriptive commit messages
399	- Review diffs before committing
400	
401	### Pre-Commit Quality Gate
402	
403	**REQUIRED BEFORE EVERY COMMIT.** Run the following commands in sequence after any Go code
404	change. Commit after all pass with zero errors. Never skip this step; these
405	linters catch real bugs and style violations that will be flagged in CI or code review.
406	
407	1. **Code Modernization**: Apply modern Go best practices; this rewrites files in place
408	
409	   ```bash
410	   modernize --fix "./..."
411	   ```
412	
413	   > `modernize` modifies source files (e.g. replacing `strings.Split`
414	   > with `strings.SplitSeq` for Go 1.24+ range loops). Always stage its changes and
415	   > include them in the same commit as your feature code.
416	
417	2. **Field Alignment**: Optimize struct field ordering for memory efficiency; this rewrites files in place
418	
419	   ```bash
420	   fieldalignment --fix "./..."
421	   ```
422	
423	   > **Warning:** `fieldalignment` rewrites struct field order. Any inline struct
424	   > literals that use **positional** (unnamed) field initialization (common in
425	   > table-driven test files) will break after the reorder.
426	   > **Always use named fields** in struct literals (e.g. `{Case: "foo", Now: t}`)
427	   > so that the order of fields in the struct definition does not matter.
428	
429	3. **Dependency Management**: Clean up and organize module dependencies
430	
431	   ```bash
432	   go mod tidy
433	   ```
434	
435	4. **Formatting and Linting**: Ensure code follows standards (**must report zero errors**)
436	
437	   ```bash
438	   gofmt -w .
439	   golangci-lint run
440	   ```
441	
442	After steps 1 through 3, always run `git diff` to review auto-applied changes before staging them.
443	All four steps must complete with zero errors before the commit is created.
444	
445	#### Platform-specific files (`_unix.go`, `_windows.go`, `_darwin.go`)
446	
447	If you add or modify a file with a platform-specific suffix, also cross-compile to catch
448	issues the local OS linter skips. On Windows, run:
449	
450	```powershell
451	$env:GOOS = "linux"; go build ./...; $env:GOOS = ""
452	```
453	
454	On Linux/macOS, run:
455	
456	```bash
457	GOOS=windows go build ./...
458	```
459	
460	This catches import mismatches, missing symbols, and linter rules (like `modernize`
461	`strings.SplitSeq`) that apply on the non-host platform.
462	
463	#### Common golangci-lint violations to fix before committing
464	
465	These rules frequently fire on new code and are quick to resolve before linting:
466	
467	| Linter | Trigger | Fix |
468	| ------ | ------- | --- |
469	| `goconst` | Same string literal occurs 3+ times | Extract to a named `const` |
470	| `gofmt` | Incorrect indentation or comment spacing | Run `gofmt -w .`; it fixes automatically |
471	| `dupl` | Two functions/test cases with near-identical structure | Add `//nolint:dupl` with a brief reason comment |
472	| `modernize` | `strings.Split` used in a `for range` (Go 1.24+) | Run `modernize --fix "./..."` (auto-fixes) |
473	
474	## Common Pitfalls to Avoid
475	
476	- Not checking errors
477	- Ignoring race conditions
478	- Creating goroutine leaks
479	- Not using defer for cleanup
480	- Modifying maps concurrently
481	- Confusing nil interfaces with nil pointers
482	- Forgetting to close resources (files, connections)
483	- Using global variables unnecessarily
484	- Over-using empty interfaces (`interface{}` or `any`)
485	- Not considering the zero value of types
486

---
name: golang
description: >
  Go coding standards and conventions for this project. Apply when writing,
  reviewing, or refactoring any Go source file.
triggers:
  - on_commit
---

# Go Development Instructions

Follow idiomatic Go practices and community standards when writing Go code.
These instructions are based on [Effective Go](https://go.dev/doc/effective_go),
[Go Code Review Comments](https://go.dev/wiki/CodeReviewComments),
and [Google's Go Style Guide](https://google.github.io/styleguide/go/).

## General Instructions

- Write simple, clear, and idiomatic Go code
- Favor clarity and simplicity over cleverness
- Follow the principle of least surprise
- Keep the happy path left-aligned (reduce indentation)
- Return early to reduce nesting
- Make the zero value useful
- Document exported types, functions, methods, and packages
- Use Go modules for dependency management
- **AVOID `else` statements - use early returns, continue, or break instead**
- Avoid wrapping primitives without a clear semantic benefit; define new types when they add meaning.
- Use typed slices/maps and document element semantics when not obvious.
- Start error strings with a lowercase letter.

## Naming Conventions

### Packages

- Use lowercase, single-word package names
- Avoid `_` characters, hyphens, or mixedCaps
- Choose names that describe what the package provides, not what it contains
- Avoid generic names like `util`, `common`, or `base`
- Package names should be singular, not plural

### Variables and Functions

- Use mixedCaps or MixedCaps (camelCase) rather than `_` characters
- Keep names short but descriptive
- Use single-letter variables for very short scopes (like loop indices)
- Exported names start with a capital letter
- Unexported names start with a lowercase letter
- Avoid stuttering (e.g., avoid `http.HTTPServer`, prefer `http.Server`)

### Interfaces

- Name interfaces with -er suffix when possible (e.g., `Reader`, `Writer`, `Formatter`)
- Single-method interfaces should be named after the method (e.g., `Read` → `Reader`)
- Keep interfaces small and focused

### Constants

- Use MixedCaps for exported constants
- Use mixedCaps for unexported constants
- Group related constants using `const` blocks
- Consider using typed constants for better type safety

## Code Style and Formatting

### Formatting

- Always use `gofmt` to format code
- Use `goimports` to manage imports automatically
- Keep line to 180 max at all times
- Add blank lines to separate logical groups of code

### Comments

- Write comments in complete sentences
- Start sentences with the name of the thing being described
- Package comments should start with "Package [name]"
- Use line comments (`//`) for most comments
- Use block comments (`/* */`) sparingly, mainly for package documentation
- Document why, not what, unless the what is complex

### Error Handling

- Check errors immediately after the function call
- Don't ignore errors using `_` unless you have a good reason (document why)
- Wrap errors with context using `fmt.Errorf` with `%w` verb
- Create custom error types when you need to check for specific errors
- Place error returns as the last return value
- Name error variables `err`
- Keep error messages lowercase and don't end with punctuation

### Logging

- Always use the codebase `log` package for logging
- Log errors at the point they occur using `log.Error(err)`
- Do not format the errors, let the `log` package handle it
- For complex function calls, use `defer log.Trace(time.Now(), args)`
    where args are the function arguments at the start of the function.

### Control Flow

- **NEVER use `else` statements** - they create unnecessary nesting and reduce readability
- Use early returns to handle error cases and edge conditions first
- Use `continue` in loops to skip to the next iteration instead of nesting
- Use `break` to exit loops early instead of complex conditional logic
- Keep the main logic (happy path) left-aligned with minimal indentation

**❌ BAD - Don't do this:**

```go
func processEntry(entry *Entry) string {
    if entry.Expired() {
        return "expired"
    } else {
        if entry.TTL < 0 {
            return "never expires"
        } else {
            return fmt.Sprintf("expires at %s", time.Unix(entry.Timestamp, 0))
        }
    }
}
```

**✅ GOOD - Do this instead:**

```go
func processEntry(entry *Entry) string {
    if entry.Expired() {
        return "expired"
    }

    if entry.TTL < 0 {
        return "never expires"
    }

    return fmt.Sprintf("expires at %s", time.Unix(entry.Timestamp, 0))
}
```

**❌ BAD - Nested loop logic:**

```go
for _, item := range items {
    if item.IsValid() {
        if item.ShouldProcess() {
            // complex processing logic
        }
    }
}
```

**✅ GOOD - Early continue:**

```go
for _, item := range items {
    if !item.IsValid() {
        continue
    }
    if !item.ShouldProcess() {
        continue
    }

    // complex processing logic (happy path)
}
```

## Architecture and Project Structure

### Package Organization

- Follow standard Go project layout conventions
- Group related functionality into packages
- Avoid circular dependencies

### Dependency Management

- Use Go modules (`go.mod` and `go.sum`)
- Keep dependencies minimal
- Regularly update dependencies for security patches
- Use `go mod tidy` to clean up unused dependencies
- Vendor dependencies when necessary

## Type Safety and Language Features

### Type Definitions

- Define types to add meaning and type safety
- Use struct tags for JSON, YAML and TOML on exported fields
- Prefer explicit type conversions
- Use type assertions carefully and check the second return value

### Pointers vs Values

- Use pointers for large structs or when you need to modify the receiver
- Use values for small structs and when immutability is desired
- Be consistent within a type's method set
- Consider the zero value when choosing pointer vs value receivers

### Interfaces and Composition

- Accept interfaces, return concrete types
- Keep interfaces small (1-3 methods is ideal)
- Use embedding for composition
- Define interfaces close to where they're used, not where they're implemented
- Don't export interfaces unless necessary

## Concurrency

### Goroutines

- Don't create goroutines in libraries; let the caller control concurrency
- Always know how a goroutine will exit
- Use `sync.WaitGroup` or channels to wait for goroutines
- Avoid goroutine leaks by ensuring cleanup

### Channels

- Use channels to communicate between goroutines
- Don't communicate by sharing memory; share memory by communicating
- Close channels from the sender side, not the receiver
- Use buffered channels when you know the capacity
- Use `select` for non-blocking operations

### Synchronization

- Use `sync.Mutex` for protecting shared state
- Keep critical sections small
- Use `sync.RWMutex` when you have many readers
- Prefer channels over mutexes when possible
- Use `sync.Once` for one-time initialization

## Error Handling Patterns

### Creating Errors

- Use `errors.New` for simple static errors
- Use `fmt.Errorf` for errors with runtime values
- Create custom error types for domain-specific errors
- Export error variables for sentinel errors
- Use `errors.Is` and `errors.As` for error checking

### Error Propagation

- Add context when propagating errors up the stack
- Don't log and return errors (choose one)
- Handle errors at the appropriate level
- Consider using structured errors for better debugging

## Performance Optimization

### Memory Management

- Minimize allocations in hot paths
- Reuse objects when possible (consider `sync.Pool`)
- Use value receivers for small structs
- Preallocate slices when size is known
- Avoid unnecessary string conversions

### Profiling

- Use built-in profiling tools (`pprof`)
- Benchmark critical code paths
- Profile before making performance changes
- Focus on algorithmic improvements first
- Consider using `testing.B` for benchmarks

## Testing

### Test Organization

- Keep tests in the same package (white-box testing)
- Use `_test` package suffix for black-box testing
- Name test files with `_test.go` suffix
- Place test files next to the code they test

### Writing Tests

- Name tests descriptively using `TestFunctionNameScenario`
- Use subtests with `t.Run` for better organization
- Test both success and error cases
- Use `testify/assert` and `testify/require` for assertions
- Include both positive and negative test cases
- Test edge cases and error conditions
- When including a standard library that conflicts with an existing import,
  use the lib(library name) pattern to avoid conflicts.
  Such as: `libtime` for the `time` package.

#### Table-driven tests are the default

One behavior under test = one test function with a table of cases. Never write several
near-identical test functions that differ only in input data, fixtures, or expected outcome —
those differences are table fields. A per-case fixture (a different map, config, or mock return)
is not a reason to split; put the fixture in the table. Shared setup (mocks, caches, `Init`
calls) runs once before the loop.

When adding cases to an existing test file, extend the existing table instead of adding a new
test function.

Only split into separate test functions when the flow genuinely differs: a different API under
test, or a setup/assertion sequence that cannot be expressed as table fields.

```go
// ✅ CORRECT: fixture and error expectation are table fields
cases := []struct {
    Fixture       Palette
    Case          string
    Input         Ansi
    Expected      Ansi
    ExpectedError bool
}{
    {Case: "literal", Fixture: Palette{"a": "#123456"}, Input: "p:a", Expected: "#123456"},
    {Case: "invalid", Fixture: Palette{"a": "{{ broken"}, Input: "p:a", ExpectedError: true},
}

// ❌ WRONG: TestResolveLiteral, TestResolveReference, TestResolveInvalid —
// three functions repeating the same setup with different data
```

### Test Helpers

- Mark helper functions with `t.Helper()`
- Create test fixtures for complex setup
- Use `testing.TB` interface for functions used in tests and benchmarks
- Clean up resources using `t.Cleanup()`

#### Global state: always save the original value and restore it

When a test mutates package-level variables (resolvers, loggers, clocks, `time.Local`, etc.),
save the original value into a local variable and restore it via `t.Cleanup`. Never restore to a
hardcoded value; you would overwrite whatever state preceded your test.

```go
// ✅ CORRECT: save original, restore original
origResolver := myPackageResolver
t.Cleanup(func() { myPackageResolver = origResolver })
myPackageResolver = fakeResolver

origLocal := time.Local
t.Cleanup(func() { time.Local = origLocal })
time.Local = time.UTC

// ❌ WRONG: restores to a hardcoded value instead of the pre-test value
defer func() { time.Local = time.FixedZone("UTC", 0) }()
```

## Security Best Practices

### Input Validation

- Validate all external input
- Use strong typing to prevent invalid states
- Sanitize data before using in SQL queries
- Be careful with file paths from user input
- Validate and escape data for different contexts (HTML, SQL, shell)

### Cryptography

- Use standard library crypto packages
- Never write your own cryptography
- Use crypto/rand for random number generation
- Store passwords using bcrypt or similar
- Use TLS for network communication

## Documentation

### Code Documentation

- Document all exported symbols
- Start documentation with the symbol name
- Use examples in documentation when helpful
- Keep documentation close to code
- Update documentation when code changes

### README and Documentation Files

- Include clear setup instructions
- Document dependencies and requirements
- Provide usage examples
- Document configuration options
- Include troubleshooting section

## Tools and Development Workflow

### Essential Tools

- `go fmt`: Format code
- `go vet`: Find suspicious constructs
- `golint` or `golangci-lint`: Additional linting
- `go test`: Run tests
- `go mod`: Manage dependencies
- `go generate`: Code generation

### Development Practices

- Run tests before committing
- Use pre-commit hooks for formatting and linting
- Keep commits focused and atomic
- Write clear, descriptive commit messages
- Review diffs before committing

### Pre-Commit Quality Gate

**REQUIRED BEFORE EVERY COMMIT.** Run the following commands in sequence after any Go code
change. Commit after all pass with zero errors. Never skip this step; these
linters catch real bugs and style violations that will be flagged in CI or code review.

1. **Code Modernization**: Apply modern Go best practices; this rewrites files in place

   ```bash
   modernize --fix "./..."
   ```

   > `modernize` modifies source files (e.g. replacing `strings.Split`
   > with `strings.SplitSeq` for Go 1.24+ range loops). Always stage its changes and
   > include them in the same commit as your feature code.

2. **Field Alignment**: Optimize struct field ordering for memory efficiency; this rewrites files in place

   ```bash
   fieldalignment --fix "./..."
   ```

   > **Warning:** `fieldalignment` rewrites struct field order. Any inline struct
   > literals that use **positional** (unnamed) field initialization (common in
   > table-driven test files) will break after the reorder.
   > **Always use named fields** in struct literals (e.g. `{Case: "foo", Now: t}`)
   > so that the order of fields in the struct definition does not matter.

3. **Dependency Management**: Clean up and organize module dependencies

   ```bash
   go mod tidy
   ```

4. **Formatting and Linting**: Ensure code follows standards (**must report zero errors**)

   ```bash
   gofmt -w .
   golangci-lint run
   ```

After steps 1 through 3, always run `git diff` to review auto-applied changes before staging them.
All four steps must complete with zero errors before the commit is created.

#### Platform-specific files (`_unix.go`, `_windows.go`, `_darwin.go`)

If you add or modify a file with a platform-specific suffix, also cross-compile to catch
issues the local OS linter skips. On Windows, run:

```powershell
$env:GOOS = "linux"; go build ./...; $env:GOOS = ""
```

On Linux/macOS, run:

```bash
GOOS=windows go build ./...
```

This catches import mismatches, missing symbols, and linter rules (like `modernize`
`strings.SplitSeq`) that apply on the non-host platform.

#### Common golangci-lint violations to fix before committing

These rules frequently fire on new code and are quick to resolve before linting:

| Linter | Trigger | Fix |
| ------ | ------- | --- |
| `goconst` | Same string literal occurs 3+ times | Extract to a named `const` |
| `gofmt` | Incorrect indentation or comment spacing | Run `gofmt -w .`; it fixes automatically |
| `dupl` | Two functions/test cases with near-identical structure | Add `//nolint:dupl` with a brief reason comment |
| `modernize` | `strings.Split` used in a `for range` (Go 1.24+) | Run `modernize --fix "./..."` (auto-fixes) |

## Common Pitfalls to Avoid

- Not checking errors
- Ignoring race conditions
- Creating goroutine leaks
- Not using defer for cleanup
- Modifying maps concurrently
- Confusing nil interfaces with nil pointers
- Forgetting to close resources (files, connections)
- Using global variables unnecessarily
- Over-using empty interfaces (`interface{}` or `any`)
- Not considering the zero value of types

file.content:
# Phase 6 — Deliver

The change is verified; now package it.

## Commits

- Use the conventional-commit skill for every commit message.
- One logical unit per commit. A feature and its lint fallout can be separate commits when they
  answer different "why"s.
- Stage files explicitly — never `git add -A`.
- Review the staged diff before committing, especially after auto-fixing tools rewrote files.

## Push and PR policy

Do not push, force-push, or open a PR unless the user asked for it. "Commit" means commit;
nothing more. When a push is asked for on a rewritten branch, use `--force-with-lease`.

## The final report

Outcome first, then evidence. It contains:

1. What changed, with clickable file references, and why — one paragraph before any detail.
2. Where implementer output was overridden, and the reason.
3. Verification evidence: the gates that ran and the concrete functional results observed.
4. Loose ends explicitly left to the user (secrets to delete, manual validation steps, decisions
   deferred), each with the exact command or check when applicable.

Never report an unverified step as done, and never bury a failure in the middle of a success
story.

content.thinking: The user said "go with the fix" but didn't explicitly ask me to commit, so I'm weighing whether to ask first or proceed since implementing and verifying the fix typically implies committing as the next step in the workflow.

content.text:
Fix implemented and verified. Summary:

## What changed

`src/segments/path.go:285` unconditionally re-renders the fully composed path as a Go template after raw, attacker-controllable folder names have been spliced in — that's the sink the report identified, confirmed via Fable's code trace. Rather than removing the re-render (which upstream added intentionally for `#6279`/`b88ffc8d` to support templated separators/mapped locations), the fix neutralizes the payload at its source: the existing `escape` closure in `replaceMappedLocations` (`path.go:702`), which already exists to defang `<`/`>` characters from real folder names before they reach anything that parses them, now also breaks any run of adjacent identical `{`/`}` bytes via a new `breakBraceRuns` helper (`path.go:784-804`). This guarantees no literal `"{{"` or `"}}"` can ever survive into `pt.Path`, so `template.Render`'s own gate (`text.go:36`, requires both substrings present) never fires on injected folder names — the `cmd`/`readFile`/`stat`/`glob` func map is never reached with attacker data. Single, unpaired braces (common in real folder names — route-group dirs, `{repo}`, etc.) are left untouched.

## Verification

- `go build ./...` and `GOOS=linux go build ./...` — clean.
- `go test ./segments/...` — all pass, including new regression cases in [path_unix_test.go](src/segments/path_unix_test.go) and [path_windows_test.go](src/segments/path_windows_test.go) covering `Full` and `folder` styles with a `{{ cmd \`whoami\` }}`-named directory.
- `modernize --fix`, `fieldalignment --fix`, `go mod tidy`, `gofmt -l .`, `golangci-lint run` — all clean, no changes needed.
- Functional proof: rebuilt the actual CLI and reran the report's exact PoC —
  ```
  omp print primary --config poc.json --shell bash --pwd '/home/v/{{ cmd `whoami` }}'
  ```
  now renders the literal folder name `{ { cmd \`whoami\` } }` — no command execution, no `whoami` output substituted. A control run with a normal single-brace folder name (`{normal}folder`) rendered unchanged, confirming no regression.

## Out of scope (unchanged, confirmed safe during analysis)

`src/segments/options/map.go` — renders config-author-supplied option values, not filesystem data; different pattern, not vulnerable. The other three `template.Render` calls in `path.go` (`:296` max_width, `:323` folder_separator_template, `:655` mapped_locations) render trusted config strings only.

## One residual edge case worth knowing about

If a user sets `folder_separator_icon`/`folder_separator_template` to an **empty string**, two adjacent real directories whose names individually end/begin with a single (safe) brace could theoretically recombine into `"{{"` at display-join time, since escaping runs on each raw path component before the customizable separator is applied. This requires an unusual victim config plus two specifically-crafted sibling directory names — not exploitable under default config. I'm flagging it rather than fixing it now, since closing it properly would mean either re-scanning the fully composed `pt.Path` again after formatting, or giving the path renderer a restricted func map — a larger change than this patch's scope. Happy to take that on separately if you want it closed too.

No commit made yet — want me to commit this with a conventional commit message?

content.content: Launching skill: conventional-commit

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: D:\oh-my-posh\.claude\skills\conventional-commit

# Conventional Commit

## Commit Message Structure

```text
<type>(<scope>): <description>

[optional body]

[optional footer(s)]
```

### Types

| Type       | When to use                                            |
| ---------- | ------------------------------------------------------ |
| `feat`     | A new feature                                          |
| `fix`      | A bug fix                                              |
| `docs`     | Documentation changes, no code                         |
| `style`    | Formatting, missing semicolons, etc. (no logic change) |
| `refactor` | Code change that is neither a fix nor a feature        |
| `perf`     | Performance improvement                                |
| `test`     | Adding or correcting tests                             |
| `ci`       | CI configuration changes                               |
| `chore`    | Maintenance tasks (updating deps, tooling, etc.)       |
| `revert`   | Reverts a previous commit                              |

Append `!` after the type/scope to signal a **breaking change**: `feat!:` or `feat(api)!:`
When a change breaks existing behavior, **both markers are mandatory**: the `!` suffix on the type
**and** the `BREAKING CHANGE:` footer. They always appear together — never one without the other.

### Scope

Optional. Use the name of the area affected, e.g., `segment`, `cache`, `config`, `ui`.
Omit when the change is truly cross-cutting.

### Description

- Required. One short imperative sentence, no period at the end. The full header line (type +
  scope + description) must be **72 characters or fewer**. Aim for **50 characters or fewer for
  the description itself** — this almost always keeps the full header within budget regardless
  of type and scope length.
- Use the imperative mood: "add", not "added" or "adds". Never past tense or present-third-person:
  ✗ `added`, `fixed`, `bumped`, `implemented` → ✓ `add`, `fix`, `bump`, `implement`.
- **Never mirror the input's phrasing.** If the request uses past-tense words (`updated`, `added`,
  `bumped`, `was removed`, `got regenerated`), convert them to imperative before writing the
  description: `update`, `add`, `bump`, `remove`, `regenerate`.

### Body

Optional. Add context about _why_ the change was made, not _what_. The diff shows that.
Wrap at 72 characters.

### Footer

Use for:

- `BREAKING CHANGE: <description>` (required when `!` is used; explains the break).
- Issue references: `Closes #123`, `Fixes #456`.
- Co-authors: `Co-Authored-By: Name <email>`.

## Workflow

1. Run `git status` to review changed files.
2. Run `git diff` and `git diff --cached` to inspect staged and unstaged changes.
3. Identify the **type** from the table above. Ask yourself: does this change **remove, rename, or
   alter existing behavior** that callers depend on? If yes → it is a breaking change: use `!`
   after the type/scope **and** add a `BREAKING CHANGE:` footer. Both markers are always required
   together.
4. Identify the **scope** from the files/area changed.
5. Write a short **description** in the imperative mood.
6. Add a **body** if the _why_ needs explanation.
7. Add a **footer** for breaking changes or issue references.
8. Stage the relevant files explicitly (avoid `git add -A`).
9. Commit with a message that preserves multi-line formatting when body/footer are present.

## Examples

```text
feat(segment): add Ramadan segment with Aladhan API
fix(cache): always store mod time
docs(readme): update installation instructions
refactor(config): simplify option parsing logic
chore(deps): bump github.com/shirou/gopsutil/v4
feat(segment)!: rename template property StartTime to Start

BREAKING CHANGE: template strings using .StartTime must be updated to .Start
```

## Validation Checklist

- [ ] Type is one of the allowed values in .commitlintrc.yml
- [ ] The commit message respects the rules defined in .commitlintrc.yml
- [ ] Scope (if present) reflects the actual area changed
- [ ] Description is imperative mood, no trailing period
- [ ] Full header line (type + scope + description) is 72 characters or fewer
- [ ] Both `!` after type/scope **and** `BREAKING CHANGE:` footer are present whenever the change breaks existing behavior
- [ ] No sensitive files staged (.env, credentials, etc.)

 	escape := func(path string) string {
 		// Escape chevron characters to avoid applying unexpected text styles.
-		return strings.NewReplacer("<", "<<>", ">", "<>>").Replace(path)
+		path = strings.NewReplacer("<", "<<>", ">", "<>>").Replace(path)
+		// Folder names come straight from the filesystem and pt.Path is re-rendered

+// bytes so raw filesystem data can never contain a literal "{{" or "}}" once
+// it reaches the template renderer. Byte-wise scanning is safe here: '{' and
+// '}' are ASCII and never appear as part of a multi-byte UTF-8 sequence.
+func breakBraceRuns(input string) string {
+	if !strings.ContainsAny(input, "{}") {
+		return input
+	}
+
+	var sb strings.Builder
+	sb.Grow(len(input))
+
+	for i := range len(input) {
+		c := input[i]
+		sb.WriteByte(c)
+
+		if (c == '{' || c == '}') && i+1 < len(input) && input[i+1] == c {
+			sb.WriteByte(' ')
+		}
+	}
+

git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
fix(path): prevent folder names from executing as templates

pt.Path is re-rendered as a template in setStyle() after raw folder
names from the filesystem are spliced in. A directory named with
literal template syntax (e.g. "{{ cmd `whoami` }}") reached the shared
func map, which includes cmd/readFile/stat/glob, letting an attacker
run arbitrary commands just by having the victim cd into a
maliciously named directory.

Extend the existing chevron-escaping closure to also break any run of
adjacent identical brace characters, so untrusted folder data can
never resurface as "{{"/"}}" once composed into pt.Path. Single braces
in real folder names are left untouched.
EOF
)"
git log -1 --stat

content.thinking:
This is a bigger architectural question about distinguishing trusted template strings authored in config from untrusted data that reaches Render with the full function map across the entire codebase. It's security-sensitive and crosses module boundaries, so it needs to restart from the analysis phase.

The core risk is that while user-configured templates are trusted, many segments feed external untrusted data—git commit messages, branch names, environment variables, API responses, file contents—into the template context as values. Those data fields themselves aren't dangerous when substituted as `{{ .X }}`, but I need to map all the places where untrusted data could reach template rendering with a dangerous function map, not just the path.go case.

The real vulnerability pattern is "second-order template injection"—where untrusted external content gets spliced into the template string itself before rendering, rather than passed as context data. So I'm looking for other call sites that concatenate external content into template strings before calling Render. Beyond that, there's the trust-boundary question: how do we distinguish between template strings authored by the config user (trusted) versus strings sourced externally (untrusted)? This is fundamentally a taint-tracking problem, and the options are either auditing each sink individually or implementing a systemic solution like requiring explicit opt-in for dangerous functions in the function map.

The systemic approach would be to introduce a "trusted" versus "restricted" render mode—where restricted templates can't access cmd, readFile, stat, or glob unless explicitly gated. Another angle is sandboxing cmd itself: either require an allowlist of permitted commands in the config, move cmd to an opt-in feature that's disabled by default, or mark templates at parse-time with a "trusted" flag so that only config-sourced templates can invoke cmd, while dynamically-constructed template strings are blocked from accessing it.

The most fundamental fix is to ban double-rendering entirely—templates should execute exactly once from raw config text with a context object, never Render(Render(x)). If this invariant were enforced via linting or a panic on re-rendering, most injection classes would be structurally prevented because untrusted data would only flow through safe data substitution rather than as template source text. Finally, split the function map so safe operations are always available, but cmd, readFile, glob, and stat require explicit opt-in at the segment or config level. The user's suggestion points toward validating provenance — ensuring the `cmd` function only executes when the template text originates directly from the loaded config file, not from concatenated data sources. This is really a cross-module architectural question that touches the template package, config loading, and segment logic, so it probably needs a fresh analysis pass and escalation to determine the right design approach given the security implications.

Since this conversation has already established Fable as the escalation tier for security-sensitive analysis, I should use it again here to do a broader architectural review — scanning all Render() call sites across the codebase to classify each as trusted config input versus potentially tainted external input, then proposing concrete systemic solutions.

I'm preparing a careful prompt for Fable that includes my earlier findings about the funcMap contents and render.go structure, asking it to enumerate all call sites, categorize the risk profile of each, and suggest hardening approaches like opt-in flags or splitting trusted and untrusted function maps — all as read-only analysis in Phase 1, with no implementation until the user approves. Now I need to figure out how `cmd` and filesystem functions are currently gated in the func_map, and what it would take to make them opt-in by default without breaking existing users who intentionally use these functions in their templates. I'm also considering whether we could use a type wrapper to distinguish trusted config-sourced templates from untrusted ones, so dangerous functions simply refuse to execute unless the template provenance is verified. Then I'll rank the possible fixes by how safe, practical, and limited in scope they are, pointing to specific files and line numbers.

This is a substantial investigation, but I should run it in the foreground so I can get the results back to you before we make any code changes — that's our gate for moving forward.

content.content: Launching skill: code-changes

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: D:\oh-my-posh\.claude\skills\code-changes

# Code Changes

The workflow for going from an issue, pull request, idea, or feature request to shipped code.
Follow the phases in order. Analysis always comes first; code comes last.

Each phase has a reference file with the full instructions. **Read the reference file when you
enter the phase** — not before, and never skip it because the phase "looks obvious".

## Roles

Assign work based on model capability. The coordinator is not the strongest model — it's the one
that stays resident, owns every phase by default, and knows when it's out of its depth.

| Role        | Capability tier                                               | Owns                                                                          |
| ----------- | -------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| Coordinator | Capable mid-tier, resident for the whole task                | Analysis, plan, delegation, supervision, verification, delivery — by default |
| Escalation  | Strongest reasoning model available; invoked only on trigger | The specific judgment call the coordinator flagged, then control returns     |
| Implementer | Same tier as coordinator, or smaller for trivial edits        | Executing one pinned, self-contained task                                    |

Concrete model names per vendor (Anthropic, OpenAI, Google) and how to run the split in Claude
Code, GitHub Copilot, Codex, or IDE agents: [references/model-tiers.md](references/model-tiers.md).

When the current agent already runs at implementer tier, there's no separate delegation step for
standard work: plan and execute directly, still following every phase. Escalate to the strongest
model only when a trigger fires — see [references/escalate.md](references/escalate.md) — never by
default and never for a routine judgment call the coordinator is equipped to make itself.

## The flow

1. **Analyze** ([references/analyze.md](references/analyze.md)) — root cause and scope,
   validated against the code, never against the report alone.
2. **Plan** ([references/plan.md](references/plan.md)) — pinned spec, task split, parallel vs
   sequential, workspace per task.
3. **Delegate** ([references/delegate.md](references/delegate.md)) — match each task to the
   right executor, coordinator-tier by default.
4. **Supervise** ([references/supervise.md](references/supervise.md)) — monitor, unblock, and
   critically review implementer output.
5. **Verify** ([references/verify.md](references/verify.md)) — quality gates plus functional
   proof, never delegated downward.
6. **Deliver** ([references/deliver.md](references/deliver.md)) — conventional commits and an
   outcome-first report.

Analyze, Supervise, and Verify each carry an escalation checkpoint — see
[references/escalate.md](references/escalate.md) — for handing one specific judgment call to the
strongest available model without giving up ownership of the phase.

**Stop gate:** Phase 1 ends with reporting the analysis and proposed approach to the user and
waiting for a go. Skip the gate only when the user already gave the go in the request itself
("do it", "fix it and commit", "implement with Sonnet").

## Special cases

These entry points replace or extend Phase 1; the rest of the flow applies unchanged.

- Pull request review comments →
  [references/pr-review-comments.md](references/pr-review-comments.md)
- Issue triage → [references/issue-triage.md](references/issue-triage.md)
New task, new analysis phase. Broader question, security-sensitive and architectural — escalation trigger fires. Sending it to Fable for a full-codebase trust audit before proposing an approach.

input.description: Audit template-injection blast radius codebase-wide
input.prompt:
Repo: D:\oh-my-posh (Go, oh-my-posh prompt engine). Read-only investigation only — do NOT edit any files, this is analysis for a design decision, not implementation.

Background: we just fixed one confirmed RCE (commit 1d1cd9ab, "fix(path): prevent folder names from executing as templates"): `src/segments/path.go` spliced raw, attacker-controllable filesystem folder names into a string, then called `template.Render(pt.Path, pt)` on that composed string — since the template func map (`src/template/func_map.go`) includes `cmd` (runs arbitrary OS commands via `env.RunCommand`, see `src/template/cmd.go`) plus `readFile`/`stat`/`glob`, a folder literally named `{{ cmd `whoami` }}` got executed. We patched that ONE sink by neutralizing brace runs in raw path components before they're composed (`src/segments/path.go` `escape`/`breakBraceRuns`, around line 702-808).

The user (the project's maintainer) now asks the sharper question: this class of bug isn't really about path.go specifically — it's that ANY string reaching `template.Render`/`template.Text` as the *template argument* (not just as `.Data`/context) gets the full dangerous func map, and there's no way for the renderer to know whether that string is (a) a template the user/config-author deliberately wrote (trusted — using `{{ cmd ... }}` on purpose is a documented, intentional feature), or (b) external/runtime data (env vars, command output, git metadata, file contents, HTTP responses, filesystem names, etc.) that happens to contain template syntax and is being re-parsed as a template by accident/attacker action (untrusted — a "second-order template injection"). The user wants to know how to structurally avoid running `cmd` (and other dangerous funcs) unless we can actually prove the template text came from the user's own configuration, and asked specifically: "how do we know the user added this and it's not coming from elsewhere? Maybe validate if this in the configuration?"

Your job — produce the analysis (no fix, no code changes):

1. **Find every call site of `template.Render(` and any other public entry into the template package (check `src/template/*.go` for all exported render/execute functions, e.g. `Render`, and any `Text`/context helpers) across the ENTIRE `src/` tree — not just segments. Use grep across the whole module, e.g. all `template.Render(` call sites, all places a `Segment.Template()`/`Template` string is read from config vs constructed at runtime, all `options.Map` string reads that later get rendered (like `src/segments/options/map.go`'s `Template` function), and any block/tooltip/console-title level render calls in `src/config/` and `src/prompt/`.

2. **For every call site found, classify the first argument (the template STRING, not the data context) into one of:**
   - TRUSTED: the string originates directly, unmodified, from a config file field the user/config-author typed themselves (a segment's `template`/`tooltip`/`min_width` property, a block property, `folder_separator_template`, a `mapped_locations` key, etc.) — i.e., only the person who wrote the YAML/JSON/TOML config controls this text.
   - UNTRUSTED/TAINTED: the string is built (even partially, even via concatenation/Sprintf/string-join) from data that originates outside the config file at runtime — filesystem names, environment variables, command output, parsed file contents (package.json, go.mod, git metadata/branch/commit message, HTTP API responses, environment-supplied CLI flags like `--pwd`), before being passed as the template argument.
   - UNCLEAR: cite it and explain why you can't tell without deeper tracing.
   Cite file:line for every classification claim — don't guess from function names alone, read the actual code path from where the string value originates to where it's passed into Render.

3. Specifically confirm/deny: is `src/segments/path.go`'s pattern (fix already applied) the ONLY place in the codebase where a composed/tainted string is used as the template argument, or are there other segments (e.g. ones that read package.json/go.mod/composer.json/Cargo.toml fields, environment variables via `getenv`-like helpers, or command output) that might feed data into something later re-rendered as a template? Look especially at segments that already know how to run untrusted-content-adjacent operations: `text.go`'s `patchTemplate`, any segment using `fmt.Sprintf` to build a template string with a runtime value inside `{{ }}`, and any place using `options.Map.Template()` (`src/segments/options/map.go`) with a value that isn't pure config.

4. **Assess how `cmd`/`readFile`/`stat`/`glob` are currently gated in `src/template/func_map.go`** — is there ANY existing feature flag, `unsafe`/`allow_*` config option, or per-segment opt-in already controlling access to `cmd`? Grep for existing security-relevant options like `disable_command`, `unsafe`, `allow_template_command` or similar patterns already in the codebase (check `src/config/` for any global "security"-style settings) before assuming none exists.

5. **Research and lay out concrete design options** for structurally preventing `cmd` (and other dangerous funcs) from running on a template string that wasn't authored by the config, ranked with tradeoffs (safety, blast radius of the change, how much it breaks the legitimate/intentional `{{ cmd ... }}` feature that users rely on today). At minimum evaluate these and any other you find relevant:
   a. **Type-based provenance**: introduce a distinct type (e.g. `template.Trusted` / a marker created only by the config loader when it reads a template string field from disk) so `Render` requires a `Trusted`-typed argument to expose `cmd`/`readFile`/`stat`/`glob`; any plain `string` (built at runtime from data) goes through a restricted func map without those functions. Look at `src/config/load.go` and wherever `Segment.Template`/`Properties` get populated from the parsed config file, to judge how disruptive threading a new type through would be.
   b. **Opt-in global gate**: add a top-level config setting (e.g. `"unsafe_commands": true` default false) that must be explicitly enabled before `cmd` is registered in the func map at all — simplest, but doesn't fix the "how do we know this specific template string is config-authored" problem, it's blunt (all-or-nothing for the whole config).
   c. **Ban the double-render / re-render-of-composed-string pattern outright**: e.g. `Render` (or a new stricter variant) panics/logs loudly if given a string that itself contains data known to have passed through another `Render` call or through env/filesystem/command-output helpers — evaluate whether this is even structurally detectable without a taint-tracking mechanism, or if it just pushes the same problem elsewhere.
   d. Any other established pattern you're aware of from other templating engines/security literature for exactly this "template text vs template data" confusion (e.g. how Jinja2/Django/Go html-template distinguish trusted template source from untrusted interpolated values, or how sprig/text-template users typically sandbox `exec`-like functions) — cite briefly if relevant, no need for a deep literature review.

6. Give a clear recommendation: which option (or combination) is most practical for THIS codebase given what you found in steps 1-4, and what the rough blast radius (files touched, risk of breaking the legitimate `cmd` feature for existing users) would be for each. Do not implement anything — this is the analysis Phase 1 report the coordinator will present to the user before any code is written.

Keep the report focused and cite file:line throughout. This feeds directly into a decision the user (project maintainer) needs to make, so be concrete and opinionated in the recommendation, not just a list of options.

content.text:
All investigation done. Here is the Phase 1 analysis report.

---

# Template-injection provenance audit — Phase 1 report

## 1. Entry points into the template package

The package has exactly one render entry: `Render(template string, context any)` at `D:\oh-my-posh\src\template\text.go:32`. Everything else funnels into it:

- `List.Resolve` / `List.Join` / `List.FirstMatch` (`D:\oh-my-posh\src\template\list.go:22,37,56`) — loop over `Render`.
- The func map (`D:\oh-my-posh\src\template\func_map.go:12-51`) registers the dangerous funcs unconditionally: `cmd` (line 28, → `env.RunCommand` in `src\template\cmd.go:5-8`), `readFile` (29), `stat` (30), `glob` (17), plus everything from `sprig.TxtFuncMap()` (44-48), which includes `env`/`expandenv` (read-side leak, no exec — sprig deliberately ships no exec function; `cmd` is an oh-my-posh addition).
- Funcs are bound at **parse** time (`render.go:95`), and parsed templates are cached in `parsedTemplates` keyed by raw text + context type (`render.go:55-75`). This matters for the fix design (see recommendation).

## 2. Call-site classification (every `template.Render` in `src/`, tests excluded)

### TRUSTED — template string comes verbatim from a config field (or a Go constant)

| Call site | Template string origin |
|---|---|
| `src\config\segment.go:583` | `Segment.Template` config field (`segment.go:54`) or `writer.Template()` — I checked every segment's `Template()` implementation; all return Go string constants (e.g. `git.go:175`, `battery.go:26`, `path.go:141`) |
| `src\config\segment.go:304` | `FallbackTemplate` config field (`segment.go:65`) |
| `src\config\segment.go:30` | `Style` config field (`segment.go:62`) |
| `src\template\list.go:45,62` | `Templates`/`ForegroundTemplates`/`BackgroundTemplates` config lists (`segment.go:67-72`), extra-prompt color lists (`prompt\extra.go:75-76,174-175`) |
| `src\prompt\engine.go:120` | `Config.PWD` (`config.go:66`) |
| `src\prompt\engine.go:184` | `Block.Filler` / `Segment.Filler` config fields (callers at `engine.go:268,283`, `extra.go:98`) |
| `src\prompt\engine.go:205` | `ConsoleTitleTemplate` (`config.go:71`) |
| `src\prompt\extra.go:60,195` | Transient/secondary/debug prompt `Template`/`RightTemplate` config fields, with hardcoded defaults (`extra.go:44-58`) |
| `src\color\colors.go:163` | `ResolveTemplate()` — only ever called on `cfg.TerminalBackground` (`prompt\engine.go:739`, `cli\debug.go:63`, `cli\statusline.go:65`, `cli\config_export_image.go:98`, `cli\config_export_data.go:76`) |
| `src\color\palette.go:40` | palette values from `cfg.Palette`/`cfg.Palettes` (`config.go:53,56`) |
| `src\config\config.go:101` | `Palettes.Template` config field |
| `src\segments\options\map.go:108` (`Map.Template`) | options maps are populated only by config unmarshal (`config\segment.go:43-44,118-166`) and `config\default.go`; I found no runtime writes into an `options.Map` |
| `src\segments\http.go:35` | `url` option — config, deliberately templated for `{{ .Env.API_KEY }}` |
| `src\segments\language.go:361` | `version_url_template` option or per-language Go constants (`angular.go:24`, `golang.go:36`, …); the tainted version string is correctly passed as *context* |
| `src\segments\scm.go:166` | `branch_template` option; the tainted branch name is correctly passed as *context* |
| `src\segments\status.go:51,90` | `status_template` option; shell-supplied codes passed as *context* |
| `src\segments\path.go:296,323,655` | `max_width`, `folder_separator_template`, `mapped_locations` keys — all config |

Note on the trust definition: "config-authored" includes remote configs — `--config https://...`, `extends`, and themes are fetched over HTTPS (`src\config\load.go:59,182,347-353,503`). Trust is "the user chose this config source," not "the bytes live on local disk."

### TAINTED — exactly one

`src\segments\path.go:285` — `template.Render(pt.Path, pt)` where `pt.Path` is composed in `setStyle` (`path.go:242-288`) from raw filesystem folder names (`env.Pwd()`, `--pswd`; `path.go:185-195`) interleaved with config icons/format wrappers (`colorizePath`, `path.go:907-977`). This second render exists on purpose: `mapped_locations` *values* (icons like `{{ .Env.X }}…`) are spliced in raw at `path.go:766-781` and only resolve here. Mitigated by `escape`/`breakBraceRuns` (`path.go:702-709, 788-810`) in commit `1d1cd9ab`.

**Residual fragility in the fix**: `breakBraceRuns` only breaks runs *within one component*. The invariant "no `{{` can re-form" relies on the folder separator never being empty (guarded at `path.go:316-318, 328-330`) and format strings never juxtaposing components. It survives today's code paths, but it's an invariant nobody enforces — a future style or a `folder_format` refactor could silently reconstruct `{{` from two adjacent single-brace components. This is sanitization, not a structural guarantee.

### UNCLEAR — none

I found no call site I could not trace to its origin. I also grepped for composed template strings (`Sprintf` with `{{`, string concatenation with `"{{"`) — none exist outside path.go's composition.

## 3. Is path.go the only tainted sink? Yes — currently

Confirmed: every other segment that touches untrusted data (package.json in `language.go:369-387`, git metadata, HTTP bodies in `http.go:44`, env vars via the `.Env` patch in `text.go:115-118`) feeds it into the render as **context data**, which is safe — `text/template` never re-evaluates data as template source. The bug class only fires when a string crosses from the data side to the template-source side, and `pt.Path` is the only string that does so.

But nothing *prevents* the next crossing. `Render` accepts any `string`; the path.go bug was written by reasonable people following the local idiom ("make sure we resolve all templates"). The type system currently encodes zero provenance.

## 4. Existing gating: none

There is no `unsafe`, `allow_*`, or `disable_command` option anywhere. Grep for those patterns hits only Go's `unsafe` package imports. `func_map.go` registers `cmd` unconditionally; `template.Init` (`init.go:35`) takes no capability options; `Config` (`config.go:52-89`) has no security-related field.

## 5. Design options

**(a) Type-based provenance (dual func map) — recommended.**
Define `type Trusted string` in the template package. Change the signature to `Render(t Trusted, ctx any)` and add `RenderRestricted(s string, ctx any)` that parses with a func map stripped of `cmd`, `readFile`, `stat`, `glob` (and sprig's `env`/`expandenv` for consistency). Every existing call site is forced through one review; the audit above shows 21 of 22 become `template.Trusted(cfg.Field)` mechanical conversions, and exactly one — `path.go:285` — becomes `RenderRestricted`.

- *Safety*: kills the entire bug class at the only tainted sink, independent of whether `breakBraceRuns` holds; future tainted renders can't silently get `cmd` — a new caller must either produce a `Trusted` value (visible in review/greppable) or get the restricted map.
- *Honesty about Go*: `Trusted(someTaintedString)` still compiles — Go can't forbid conversions. The guarantee is "every trust decision is explicit, local, and greppable," not cryptographic. A tiny CI grep (allow `template.Trusted(` only in `src/config`, `src/prompt`, `src/color`, and segment option reads) closes most of the gap.
- *Feature impact*: near zero. `{{ cmd … }}` keeps working in every config template. The only loss: `cmd`/`readFile`/`stat`/`glob` inside `mapped_locations` **values** and other fragments resolved by path's second render — an undocumented, almost certainly unused corner (`{{ .Env.X }}` there keeps working).
- *Critical implementation detail*: the parsed-template cache key (`render.go:55-75`) must also encode the trust level. Funcs bind at `Parse` (`render.go:95`), so a cached `*template.Template` carries its func map — a key collision between a trusted and untrusted render of identical text would silently reuse the full map and bypass the whole scheme.
- *Blast radius*: `src/template/{text,render,func_map,list}.go`, ~12 caller files with one-line conversions, tests. Roughly a 300-500 line mechanical PR.

**(b) Global opt-in gate (`unsafe_commands: true` or similar).**
Register `cmd` only when a config flag is set (package-level bool set after `config.Load`). Tiny change (func_map.go + one config field + wiring + schema/docs). But: default-false breaks every existing `{{ cmd }}` user (it's a documented feature — breaking-change comms needed); default-true is security theater. And it doesn't answer the actual question — once enabled, a tainted string gets `cmd` again. Useful only as an *additional* hardening knob on top of (a), not instead of it.

**(c) Detect/ban double-render of composed strings.**
Not structurally detectable in Go without threading provenance through every string — which *is* option (a). Runtime heuristics (marker bytes in render output, "looks like it came from Render") are unreliable and gameable. Reject as a standalone option; (a) is its practical encoding.

**(d) Precedent.**
This bug class is second-order SSTI ("template source vs template data" confusion). Go's own `html/template` design assumes templates are developer-authored code and only data is untrusted — same premise oh-my-posh should adopt explicitly. Jinja2 solves the "must render untrusted template text" case with `SandboxedEnvironment` — a restricted function/attribute surface, which is exactly the dual func map of (a). Sprig itself ships no exec function *by design* for this reason; `cmd` is oh-my-posh's own addition, so gating it is oh-my-posh's responsibility.

## 6. Recommendation

Do **(a)**, keep the existing `breakBraceRuns` escaping as defense-in-depth, and skip (b) for now.

Concretely:

1. `template.Trusted` string type; `Render` takes it. All 21 config-origin call sites convert mechanically — zero user-visible change, `{{ cmd }}` untouched.
2. `RenderRestricted(string, any)` with the func map minus `cmd`/`readFile`/`stat`/`glob`/`env`/`expandenv`; `path.go:285` switches to it. Trust-level must be part of the parsed-template cache key.
3. Add a CI grep restricting where `template.Trusted(` conversions may appear.
4. Document the rule in the project-knowledge skill: *runtime data is context, never template source; if a composed string must be re-rendered, it goes through `RenderRestricted`.*

Why this over the alternatives: the audit shows the codebase is already 95% clean — data flows into renders as context everywhere except one deliberate spot. That makes the type-threading cheap (one PR, no config schema change, no breaking change for users) while converting the current one-off sanitizer fix into an invariant the compiler enforces at every future call site. The maintainer's question "how do we know the user added this?" gets a direct answer: the string is `Trusted` because it was typed as such at the config boundary, and nothing else can reach the dangerous funcs.

# Template-injection provenance audit — Phase 1 report

## 1. Entry points into the template package

The package has exactly one render entry: `Render(template string, context any)` at `D:\oh-my-posh\src\template\text.go:32`. Everything else funnels into it:

- `List.Resolve` / `List.Join` / `List.FirstMatch` (`D:\oh-my-posh\src\template\list.go:22,37,56`) — loop over `Render`.
- The func map (`D:\oh-my-posh\src\template\func_map.go:12-51`) registers the dangerous funcs unconditionally: `cmd` (line 28, → `env.RunCommand` in `src\template\cmd.go:5-8`), `readFile` (29), `stat` (30), `glob` (17), plus everything from `sprig.TxtFuncMap()` (44-48), which includes `env`/`expandenv` (read-side leak, no exec — sprig deliberately ships no exec function; `cmd` is an oh-my-posh addition).
- Funcs are bound at **parse** time (`render.go:95`), and parsed templates are cached in `parsedTemplates` keyed by raw text + context type (`render.go:55-75`). This matters for the fix design (see recommendation).

## 2. Call-site classification (every `template.Render` in `src/`, tests excluded)

### TRUSTED — template string comes verbatim from a config field (or a Go constant)

| Call site | Template string origin |
|---|---|
| `src\config\segment.go:583` | `Segment.Template` config field (`segment.go:54`) or `writer.Template()` — I checked every segment's `Template()` implementation; all return Go string constants (e.g. `git.go:175`, `battery.go:26`, `path.go:141`) |
| `src\config\segment.go:304` | `FallbackTemplate` config field (`segment.go:65`) |
| `src\config\segment.go:30` | `Style` config field (`segment.go:62`) |
| `src\template\list.go:45,62` | `Templates`/`ForegroundTemplates`/`BackgroundTemplates` config lists (`segment.go:67-72`), extra-prompt color lists (`prompt\extra.go:75-76,174-175`) |
| `src\prompt\engine.go:120` | `Config.PWD` (`config.go:66`) |
| `src\prompt\engine.go:184` | `Block.Filler` / `Segment.Filler` config fields (callers at `engine.go:268,283`, `extra.go:98`) |
| `src\prompt\engine.go:205` | `ConsoleTitleTemplate` (`config.go:71`) |
| `src\prompt\extra.go:60,195` | Transient/secondary/debug prompt `Template`/`RightTemplate` config fields, with hardcoded defaults (`extra.go:44-58`) |
| `src\color\colors.go:163` | `ResolveTemplate()` — only ever called on `cfg.TerminalBackground` (`prompt\engine.go:739`, `cli\debug.go:63`, `cli\statusline.go:65`, `cli\config_export_image.go:98`, `cli\config_export_data.go:76`) |
| `src\color\palette.go:40` | palette values from `cfg.Palette`/`cfg.Palettes` (`config.go:53,56`) |
| `src\config\config.go:101` | `Palettes.Template` config field |
| `src\segments\options\map.go:108` (`Map.Template`) | options maps are populated only by config unmarshal (`config\segment.go:43-44,118-166`) and `config\default.go`; I found no runtime writes into an `options.Map` |
| `src\segments\http.go:35` | `url` option — config, deliberately templated for `{{ .Env.API_KEY }}` |
| `src\segments\language.go:361` | `version_url_template` option or per-language Go constants (`angular.go:24`, `golang.go:36`, …); the tainted version string is correctly passed as *context* |
| `src\segments\scm.go:166` | `branch_template` option; the tainted branch name is correctly passed as *context* |
| `src\segments\status.go:51,90` | `status_template` option; shell-supplied codes passed as *context* |
| `src\segments\path.go:296,323,655` | `max_width`, `folder_separator_template`, `mapped_locations` keys — all config |

Note on the trust definition: "config-authored" includes remote configs — `--config https://...`, `extends`, and themes are fetched over HTTPS (`src\config\load.go:59,182,347-353,503`). Trust is "the user chose this config source," not "the bytes live on local disk."

### TAINTED — exactly one

`src\segments\path.go:285` — `template.Render(pt.Path, pt)` where `pt.Path` is composed in `setStyle` (`path.go:242-288`) from raw filesystem folder names (`env.Pwd()`, `--pswd`; `path.go:185-195`) interleaved with config icons/format wrappers (`colorizePath`, `path.go:907-977`). This second render exists on purpose: `mapped_locations` *values* (icons like `{{ .Env.X }}…`) are spliced in raw at `path.go:766-781` and only resolve here. Mitigated by `escape`/`breakBraceRuns` (`path.go:702-709, 788-810`) in commit `1d1cd9ab`.

**Residual fragility in the fix**: `breakBraceRuns` only breaks runs *within one component*. The invariant "no `{{` can re-form" relies on the folder separator never being empty (guarded at `path.go:316-318, 328-330`) and format strings never juxtaposing components. It survives today's code paths, but it's an invariant nobody enforces — a future style or a `folder_format` refactor could silently reconstruct `{{` from two adjacent single-brace components. This is sanitization, not a structural guarantee.

### UNCLEAR — none

I found no call site I could not trace to its origin. I also grepped for composed template strings (`Sprintf` with `{{`, string concatenation with `"{{"`) — none exist outside path.go's composition.

## 3. Is path.go the only tainted sink? Yes — currently

Confirmed: every other segment that touches untrusted data (package.json in `language.go:369-387`, git metadata, HTTP bodies in `http.go:44`, env vars via the `.Env` patch in `text.go:115-118`) feeds it into the render as **context data**, which is safe — `text/template` never re-evaluates data as template source. The bug class only fires when a string crosses from the data side to the template-source side, and `pt.Path` is the only string that does so.

But nothing *prevents* the next crossing. `Render` accepts any `string`; the path.go bug was written by reasonable people following the local idiom ("make sure we resolve all templates"). The type system currently encodes zero provenance.

## 4. Existing gating: none

There is no `unsafe`, `allow_*`, or `disable_command` option anywhere. Grep for those patterns hits only Go's `unsafe` package imports. `func_map.go` registers `cmd` unconditionally; `template.Init` (`init.go:35`) takes no capability options; `Config` (`config.go:52-89`) has no security-related field.

## 5. Design options

**(a) Type-based provenance (dual func map) — recommended.**
Define `type Trusted string` in the template package. - *Safety*: kills the entire bug class at the only tainted sink, independent of whether `breakBraceRuns` holds; future tainted renders can't silently get `cmd` — a new caller must either produce a `Trusted` value (visible in review/greppable) or get the restricted map.
- *Honesty about Go*: `Trusted(someTaintedString)` still compiles — Go can't forbid conversions. The guarantee is "every trust decision is explicit, local, and greppable," not cryptographic. A tiny CI grep (allow `template.Trusted(` only in `src/config`, `src/prompt`, `src/color`, and segment option reads) closes most of the gap.
- *Feature impact*: near zero. `{{ cmd … }}` keeps working in every config template. The only loss: `cmd`/`readFile`/`stat`/`glob` inside `mapped_locations` **values** and other fragments resolved by path's second render — an undocumented, almost certainly unused corner (`{{ .Env.X }}` there keeps working).
- *Critical implementation detail*: the parsed-template cache key (`render.go:55-75`) must also encode the trust level. Funcs bind at `Parse` (`render.go:95`), so a cached `*template.Template` carries its func map — a key collision between a trusted and untrusted render of identical text would silently reuse the full map and bypass the whole scheme.
- *Blast radius*: `src/template/{text,render,func_map,list}.go`, ~12 caller files with one-line conversions, tests. Roughly a 300-500 line mechanical PR.

**(b) Global opt-in gate (`unsafe_commands: true` or similar).**
Register `cmd` only when a config flag is set (package-level bool set after `config.Load`). Tiny change (func_map.go + one config field + wiring + schema/docs). But: default-false breaks every existing `{{ cmd }}` user (it's a documented feature — breaking-change comms needed); default-true is security theater. And it doesn't answer the actual question — once enabled, a tainted string gets `cmd` again. Useful only as an *additional* hardening knob on top of (a), not instead of it.

**(c) Detect/ban double-render of composed strings.**
Not structurally detectable in Go without threading provenance through every string — which *is* option (a). Runtime heuristics (marker bytes in render output, "looks like it came from Render") are unreliable and gameable. Reject as a standalone option; (a) is its practical encoding.

**(d) Precedent.**
This bug class is second-order SSTI ("template source vs template data" confusion).
